The Stacey Matrix & the Zen Garden
IThe Object IIThe Sources IIIThe Politics, I IVThe Politics, II VThe Philosophy VIThe Psyche VIIThe Physics VIIIThe History IXThe Science XThe Mirror XIThe Verdict
A Critical Study · Perspective · Power · Complexity · The View From Nowhere

The Stacey Matrix& the Zen Garden

On David Hockney’s Walking in the Zen Garden at the Ryōan-ji Temple, Kyoto, the fifteen stones that cannot all be seen at once, and the matrix that reveals every chosen perspective to be a political act

A long-form study built on close readings of the NCCMT / Zimmerman teaching aide and the Praxis Framework entry; on Chris Mowles's Complexity: A Key Idea for Business and Society and Chris Rodgers's Informal Coalitions; on Stacey's primary corpus (1991–2016); and on the non-equilibrium thermodynamics of Prigogine, the social-entropy theory of Bailey, and the group-analytic matrix of Foulkes — drawn into a single argument: that the matrix's neglected vertical axis is, at root, a measure of social entropy.

Begin
Abstract

This is a study of two objects that turn out to be one. The first is a photograph that is not a photograph — David Hockney’s Walking in the Zen Garden at the Ryōan-ji Temple, Kyoto (February 1983), a “joiner” assembled from scores of separate exposures taken from scores of positions, of a fifteenth-century garden built so that from no single seat can all fifteen of its stones be seen at once. The second is the most reproduced diagram in management, the Stacey Matrix. The claim that binds them is this: there is no single place from which to view the world; every perspective is partial and situated; and to choose one perspective — above all, to present one as the neutral, complete, objective view — is a political act. The garden is a five-century-old proof of the first clause; Hockney’s joiner is its restatement in light and time; and the Stacey Matrix, read with care, makes the last clause unavoidable — for to locate a situation in a field of certainty and agreement is already to take up a position within it, and the field’s most extreme morphology, the deletion of the agreement axis, is the precise gesture by which a political act is disguised as a technical one. What follows keeps every finding of the original inquiry, but re-hangs them as a joiner: many partial views of one field, taken from many positions, none of them the whole.

This study reorganises a long inquiry into the Stacey Matrix around a single neglected element — its vertical axis, the axis of agreement — and argues that this axis is the key to everything the diagram's two hundred and forty imitators lost. The argument proceeds in three movements. First, it reads the two documents that most shaped the diagram's reception — Brenda Zimmerman's teaching aide for the National Collaborating Centre for Methods and Tools, and the Praxis Framework's project-management entry — closely enough to show what each preserved and what each began to surrender, and recovers from the Zimmerman aide two features the popular versions discard: the complete five-zone structure and Stephen Larned's seven-intervention diagonal.

Second, it isolates the political dimension that the agreement axis encodes — the recognition that disagreement is not a deficiency of information but an irreducible feature of organisations as associations of interests — and develops it through the theory of the dominant coalition (Cyert, March, Thompson); the informal coalitions, shadow side, functional politics and four forms of power set out in Chris Rodgers's book; the public and hidden transcripts of James C. Scott; the thought collectives of Ludwik Fleck; and Douglas Griffin's account of how systems thinking curdles into cult values and blame. It reads, in full, Chris Mowles's Complexity: A Key Idea for Business and Society on power, managerialism-as-ideology and the rhetoric of the view from nowhere, together with his 2026 essay on the politics of gossip and the policing of professional tribes on LinkedIn — and shows that the diagram's neglected vertical axis is precisely the instrument that explains them. The study then traces the same political dimension down to its roots in the pragmatism of Peirce, Mead and Dewey — and the sensemaking of Karl Weick, for whom organizing is the reduction of equivocality — where belief is "a form of stability and satisfaction" and doubt an "irritation" — and across to the psychodynamics of S. H. Foulkes, where the cathartic rupture that brings a hidden transcript into the open is a discharge of accumulated tension. The decisive corruption of the diagram, Ken Schwaber's 2004 renaming of the axes for Scrum, consists precisely in the deletion of this whole dimension.

Third, and at its centre, the study builds a bridge the existing literature has only gestured toward: it links the agreement axis to non-equilibrium thermodynamics. Drawing on Prigogine's dissipative structures, Bailey's social-entropy theory, and the information-theoretic account of entropy as uncertainty over states, it argues that disagreement is social entropy; that consensus is a low-entropy, high-information state purchased only by continuous work against an entropic drift; that the maintenance of agreement is therefore a thermodynamic cost borne by power; and that Stacey's "zone of complexity" is precisely a far-from-equilibrium dissipative regime — the one place where order arises through fluctuation. The reading corrects a persistent inversion: the diagram's "anarchy" corner is not chaos but thermodynamic equilibrium — the heat-death of an organisation, maximum entropy, the state in which no work can be done. The matrix, read whole, is a map of how far an organisation sits from social equilibrium, and at what cost — and by whom — it is held there.

◆
○
Overture · On the Politics of Perspective

The Joiner and the Garden

“The more affects we allow to speak about one thing, the more eyes, different eyes, we can use to observe one thing, the more complete will our ‘objectivity’ be.” — Friedrich Nietzsche

In February 1983 David Hockney knelt at the edge of the most famous rock garden in the world and refused to take its picture. He took, instead, scores of pictures — a Pentax in his hand, moving, crouching, leaning — and assembled them into a single image he called a joiner. The result has not one vanishing point but, in his own count of such works, “800 of them… That’s how we see. The camera sees geometrically, but we see psychologically.”63 Hockney had come to loathe the single-point perspective of the ordinary photograph — the “hell of one-point perspective,” he called it, that “endless regress to an infinitely distant point… where everything is hurtling away from you and you yourself are not even in the picture at all.”63 The joiner is the cure: many eyes, gathered over time, in the manner of Cubism — and, crucially, the viewer is put back into the image. Along the lower edge of the Ryōan-ji piece runs a strip of photographs of Hockney’s own feet, walking the gravel. The picture does not show the garden from a place. It shows the walk.

The Garden

What he was walking is itself an argument about perspective, five centuries old. The dry garden of Ryōan-ji holds fifteen stones on raked white gravel, and they are arranged with a precision that has become legendary: from any single seat on the veranda of the abbot’s hall, at least one stone is always hidden — no vantage point reveals all fifteen at once.64 The temple’s own gloss is exact for our purposes: fifteen is, in the old reckoning, the number of completion, and our inability ever to see all fifteen is a figure of our limited perspective.64 The garden is meant to be contemplated from a fixed seat — and that fixed seat is structurally denied the whole. Two further facts deepen the lesson. Its makers were not monks but kawaramono, riverbank labourers of the lowest caste, two of whom — Kotarō and Hikojirō — carved their names on the back of a half-buried stone: the garden’s authors are themselves its hidden sixteenth element, present and unseen.64 And its meaning has never been agreed: islands, tiger cubs, mountain peaks, pure abstraction — five centuries on, “everyone has their own interpretation.”64 A garden that cannot be wholly seen, made by hands kept out of view, meaning forever contested. Hockney’s many-eyed joiner of this place is therefore a double refusal of the single eye, two refusals laid across each other; and the walking feet supply the only resolution either offers — there is no seat that sees the whole, there is only the traverse.

There Is No View From Nowhere

This is the oldest quarrel in the theory of knowledge, and the garden has already taken a side. Nietzsche named it: “against the positivism which halts at phenomena — ‘there are only facts’ — I would say: no, facts are just what there aren’t, there are only interpretations.”65 But his perspectivism is not the lazy relativism it is mistaken for; it has a positive account of objectivity, and it is precisely Hockney’s: objectivity is not the absence of perspective but the integration of the most perspectives — “the more eyes, different eyes, we can use… the more complete will our objectivity be.”65 The joiner is that sentence made visible. Against this stands the ideal the West has chased since Descartes — what Thomas Nagel called the view from nowhere: the attempt “to view the world not from a place within it… but from nowhere in particular and no form of life in particular at all.”66 Nagel knew it was “the unreachable ideal”; Richard Rorty went further, insisting that “the image of climbing out of our minds… needs to be replaced”;66 and Donna Haraway gave the unreachable ideal its sharpest name — the “god-trick of seeing everything from nowhere” — against which she set situated knowledge, the only kind that can “become answerable for what we learn how to see” (Part I).59 The fifteenth stone is the view from nowhere: the position that would see everything, and that no one on the veranda can occupy.

You Cannot Draw a Distinction and Stand Outside It

There is a tidier way to say why. To think at all, we draw distinctions — and, as George Spencer-Brown observed in Laws of Form, “to draw a distinction is already to stand on one side of it. The moment you draw a circle on a blank page you create an inside and an outside… You cannot draw a distinction and simultaneously stand outside it.”67 Heinz von Foerster, founder of second-order cybernetics, drew the same conclusion from the other direction: “the environment as we perceive it is our invention”; observation is never passive, and “there is no neutral vantage point from which to inspect things… independent of any observer.”67 Gödel showed the limit is structural, not temporary — expand the system and the horizon moves but never vanishes — and Bateson traced the world’s deepest troubles to “the difference between how nature works and the way people think.”67,51 Which is why the managerial cliché — think outside the box — is a category error. There is no outside. As a Zen koan asks, to break exactly this assumption: “What was your face before your parents were born?” The question does not deliver a place beyond the frame; it “makes you feel the edge of the frame itself.”67 Thinking outside the box is impossible. Becoming aware of the box you are already inside is the whole of the work.

The Matrix as the Drawn Distinction

And here the Stacey Matrix walks into the garden. For the matrix is, in Spencer-Brown’s exact sense, a drawn distinction: two axes, certainty and agreement, scored across a plane to make an inside and an outside, zones and corners. To place a situation upon it is not to survey that situation from above but to take up a position within the field — to choose where, and from where, to stand. This is the hinge of the entire study, and the politics of perspective is its consequence. Every perspective privileges some positions and conceals others: the seat that hides the fifteenth stone, the labourers kept off the veranda, the eye that the single-point photograph erases from its own picture. To present one located perspective as the neutral, objective, complete view is therefore not an epistemic modesty but a political manoeuvre — it disappears the chooser and the chooser’s power. Perspectives, the political theorists insist, are learned and situated framings, not inherent facts; objectivity is reached, if at all, by integrating many affects rather than pretending an Archimedean point exists; and the framing performed by institutions, media and now algorithms is never innocent of the interests it serves.68 The matrix makes all of this legible — and its most consequential extreme morphology proves it. The deletion of the agreement axis, by which the rich field was flattened into the four-box of common use, removes the very dimension along which disagreement, position and power become visible, and re-presents a contested, perspectival situation as a single technical fact with a single correct answer. The four-box is the management diagram’s view from nowhere — a god-trick in two dimensions, the political act of erasing the agreement axis disguised, once more, as method.

How to Walk This Book

It follows that this study cannot honestly be an argument from a single standpoint, and it is not. It is a joiner. Each part that follows is a position on the veranda, a partial exposure of one field: the politics (Parts III–IV) is the seat from which power is visible; the philosophy (Part V), the seat of pragmatism and situated knowledge; the physics (Part VII), the seat from which the field reads as non-equilibrium thermodynamics; the morphology (Parts VII–IX), the seat from which it reads as a space of possible forms; the map and the territory (Part I), the seat from which it reads as the problem of representation itself. No seat sees all fifteen stones. The reader is not asked to find the one correct vantage — there is none — but to walk: to assemble, from many partial views, the only objectivity Nietzsche thought available to us, the kind made of more eyes. The Stacey Matrix and the Zen garden, it turns out, say one thing in two languages. Where you stand is never neutral. And the refusal to admit that you are standing somewhere is the oldest move in politics.

Executive Summary · for the reader in a hurry

The Argument in Brief

A two-page synopsis of an eleven-part study · read this, then dive where it draws you

The most reproduced diagram in management has been gutted of the one thing that made it true. Its vertical axis — agreement — is a measure of politics, of power, and, at root, of social entropy. This study recovers it.

The Five Claims

  1. The thing managers call “the Stacey Matrix” is a copy of a copy. Ralph Stacey drew a five-zone diagram to hold a paradox open, then disowned it; the four-box version that conquered the internet is a later mutation he never endorsed.
  2. Its two axes measure two different kinds of problem. The horizontal (certainty) is epistemic — can we know what causes what? The vertical (agreement) is political — do the people who must act want the same thing? A political problem has no epistemic cure: no amount of analysis dissolves a conflict of values.
  3. The political axis is the part everyone deleted. Stacey’s own labels read “dominant coalitions” and “ideological control”; Ken Schwaber’s 2004 Scrum version renamed the axis “requirements,” replacing a political variable with a technical one and erasing organisational power at a stroke.
  4. Disagreement is social entropy. Agreement is an improbable, low-entropy state; like any order far from equilibrium it must be paid for by continuous work — the work of power. Stacey’s “zone of complexity” is a far-from-equilibrium regime where order arises through fluctuation.
  5. The diagram’s far corner is mislabelled. “Anarchy” is not chaos but thermodynamic equilibrium — maximum entropy, the social heat-death in which no work can be done. The diagram has its danger and its life in the wrong places.

The Field, in One Reading

low SRational corner (agree + certain) — near-equilibrium order: plan, forecast, best practice. Cheap to hold.
high SPolitical zone (disagree + certain) — the facts are clear, the values are not. Order must be worked in by coalition and power.
midVision zone (agree + uncertain) — the path can’t be known, so shared purpose substitutes for the plan.
gen.Zone of complexity (the diagonal) — far-from-equilibrium and generative: the one place novelty arises, through amplified fluctuation.
max SEquilibrium corner (disagree + uncertain) — not “anarchy” but heat-death: every gradient levelled, no work possible.

Where the Evidence Comes From

The two primary documents

Zimmerman’s NCCMT teaching aide (the last faithful copy, with Larned’s seven-intervention diagonal) and the Praxis Framework entry (the first managed one).

Politics & power

Rodgers on informal coalitions and the shadow side; Scott’s public and hidden transcripts; the dominant-coalition theory of Cyert, March & Thompson.

Thought collectives

Mowles’s Complexity and his essay on LinkedIn tribalism; Fleck’s thought styles; Elias & Scotson; Griffin on cult values and blame.

The physics

Prigogine’s dissipative structures; Bailey’s social-entropy theory; Shannon’s H; with Peirce, Mead and Foulkes beneath.

In one sentence: the matrix is a map of how far an organisation sits from social equilibrium — and at what cost, and by whom, it is held there.

Contents

  1. The ArtefactWhat the diagram was, its five zones, and the paradox of control
  2. The Two Primary DocumentsThe Zimmerman aide and the Praxis entry, read closely — and the Larned diagonal recovered
  3. The Political Axis, I — Disagreement as IrreducibleElias, the dominant coalition, and the feature the imitators deleted
  4. The Political Axis, II — Coalitions, Transcripts, Thought CollectivesRodgers, Scott, Fleck, Griffin — and Mowles on the politics of LinkedIn
  5. The Pragmatist EnginePeirce's doubt and belief, Mead's living present, and how meaning is made
  6. The Psychodynamic DimensionFoulkes, catharsis, and the rupture that brings the hidden transcript into the open
  7. The Thermodynamics of DisagreementPrigogine, Bailey, Shannon — the agreement axis as social entropy · and the matrix as a possibility landscape (Wright, Kauffman, Waddington)
  8. The Genealogy, 1986–2026Pre-history, mutation, migration, disappearance, and the 240 adaptations
  9. The Edge of ChaosThe borrowed boundary, its refutation, and how chaos relates to entropy
  10. The Two MatricesThe Foulkesian web, the Cartesian grid, and their common root in Elias
  11. DeterminatioThe threshold object, and entropy as the deep unifier
I The Object · Part I

The Artefact

"A method to select the appropriate management actions in a complex adaptive system based on the degree of certainty and level of agreement on the issue in question." — Zimmerman's one-sentence statement of the diagram

The object a generation of managers calls "the Stacey Matrix" is the most reproduced and least understood artefact in the complexity-and-management literature, and the reason is that almost everyone has met it through a copy of a copy. To say anything true about it one must hold three things apart: the diagram Ralph Stacey actually drew; the documents through which it was transmitted; and the derivative economy that grew up after he disowned it. This study begins with the artefact, turns in Part II to the two documents that most shaped its reception, and builds from there — through the political dimension and the thermodynamics that underwrite it — toward a single coherent account.

The diagram first appeared in the 1993 first edition of Stacey's textbook Strategic Management and Organisational Dynamics and was elaborated in Complexity and Creativity in Organizations (1996).1 It plotted two continua. The horizontal measured certainty — the degree to which the cause-and-effect linkages bearing on a decision are known, running from "close to certainty," where the present resembles the past and extrapolation is legitimate, to "far from certainty," where the situation is novel and extrapolation misleads. The vertical measured agreement — the degree to which the people who must act share goals and methods. The first is an axis of knowledge; the second is an axis of politics. Their independence is the diagram's founding insight, and the whole of this study turns on the second axis, because it is the one the imitators lost.

A copy of a copy conquered the field — and the copy had quietly deleted the half of the map that asks something of power.

The Five Zones

The original named five regions, and the difference from the four-box internet versions is not cosmetic but substantive.

The original five-zone structure (Stacey 1993–96, as preserved by Zimmerman 2001)
Mission & Vision

High agreement, low certainty. The path cannot be known, so shared purpose substitutes for the plan; progress is judged against vision, not milestones.

The Zone of Complexity

The large central arc — moderate-to-low on both axes. Traditional management fails here; it is the region of creativity, innovation, and breaking with the past. Also called the edge of chaos.

Rational / Technical

High certainty, high agreement. The home of planning, forecasting, monitoring, best practice. The goal is to repeat what works.

Political

High certainty, low agreement. The facts are clear but the parties disagree; the work is coalition, negotiation, compromise — the management of power.

← close to certaintyfar from certainty →
The fifth zone — Anarchy — sits in the far corner (low certainty, low agreement): the region to be avoided, where planning, vision and negotiation all fail at once. Part IV argues it is misnamed.

The political zone — high certainty, low agreement — is the feature most consistently destroyed downstream, and its loss is the most damaging, because it encodes a genuinely sociological truth: that disagreement is not a deficiency of information to be cured by more analysis, but a permanent structural feature of organisations as associations of competing interests. When the agile mutation (Part V) renames the agreement axis "requirements," this zone vanishes, and the diagram becomes the politically-lobotomised descendant of a politically-literate original.

What It Was For

It was not, in 1996, a sorting device. It was a device for staging the paradox of control — for showing, on one sheet, that the rational toolkit which management treats as universal is valid only in one small zone, and that most of organisational life lies in regions that toolkit cannot reach. Stacey's later, blunt formulation of the danger in his own diagram names the stakes: it "is easily interpreted in a way that collapses the paradox of certainty and uncertainty and so sustains the dominant discourse on management while using an alternative jargon of complexity."2 The diagram was meant to hold a paradox open; the four-box matrix collapses it by implying that complexity is a property some situations have and most do not. That collapse is the subject of the rest of this study.

The Diagram as a Map — and What It Renders Invisible

Before the matrix is read for its content it should be recognised for the kind of object it is: a map. And the century-long discussion of maps, from Alfred Korzybski onward, bears on it with unusual force. Korzybski’s founding dictum — that “a map is not the territory it represents, but, if correct, it has a similar structure to the territory, which accounts for its usefulness”50 — carries three claims the diagram’s users routinely forget: that the map is not the thing; that it is useful only because of a structural correspondence; and that a perfect map is impossible, since it would have to contain a map of itself, endlessly. Korzybski’s name for the discipline of remembering this was consciousness of abstracting — the trained awareness that in every act of representation, characteristics have been left out. The four-box diagram is precisely an abstraction held without that consciousness: a map mistaken, by two hundred and forty imitators, for the territory.

Lewis Carroll gave the point its sharpest form. In Sylvie and Bruno Concluded a cartographers’ guild perfects a map “on the scale of a mile to the mile” — whereupon the farmers object that it would blot out the sun, and the country is used as its own map instead.52 The joke is a theorem: a representation that omits nothing has ceased to represent. The utility of a map is identical with its infidelity; a map is useful because it leaves things out. It follows that every framework is, in the phrase of the companion synthesis The Living Map, “a theory of salience” — a decision, usually unexamined, about what counts.54 And here that essay states, from the other direction, the indictment this study has been mounting: the Stacey Matrix “makes certainty and agreement visible and renders the relational history of the people in the room, the power dynamics that govern what may be said, and the emotional weather of the conversation, invisible.”54 The four-box mutation worsens this by an order of magnitude — it deletes one of the two things the original at least made visible, agreement, and so renders power itself unmappable.

Gregory Bateson radicalised the dictum: pushed back, the territory dissolves into “an infinite series of maps… the territory never gets in at all,”51 and what a map records is not substance but difference — famously, “a difference that makes a difference.” This is the deep reason the agreement axis cannot be recovered by adding data: agreement is not a feature of an external territory awaiting survey but a difference enacted among people (Weick’s consensual validation, Part V), and which differences register depends on who is mapping, from where. Jean Baudrillard supplied the vertiginous late turn, in which “the map precedes the territory… and engenders it”53 — the exact pathology of the franchised graphic, where teams now experience their own situation through the diagram, filing live political conflict under “complicated” because the map told them to, until the map has generated the territory it claimed only to describe. The remedy is not a better map but Whitehead’s warning, set at the head of Korzybski’s book: “a civilisation that cannot burst through its current abstractions is doomed to sterility.”50 To restore the agreement axis is to burst through the abstraction — to recover consciousness of what the diagram, as a map, must always leave out, and to feel its gravest omission, power, pressing back.

The View From Somewhere — and the Living Map

There is a trap in this critique that the companion synthesis names at the outset: everyone who writes against maps is drawing one. The theorist who rejects frameworks delivers a lecture organised by the unstated framework of rejection; a critique of the two-by-two is itself a cartography of the two-by-two’s limits.54 Chris Mowles names the underlying reflex with precision — most thinking offered to organisations concerns “maps and frameworks of a world thought to exist ‘out there’… more or less realist and representationalist.”54 The four-box diagram is the purest specimen of that realism: a picture of a system imagined to lie outside the people describing it. The lesson is not to abandon the map — that is impossible — but to change one’s relation to it, from the possession of a representation to participation in a practice; and the positive proposal of this study follows from that shift.

Donna Haraway supplies the epistemology of the shift. Against the “god-trick” of the disembodied overview — the view from nowhere that claims to see everything from no particular place — she holds that “only partial perspective promises objective vision,” a situated knowledge that “allows us to become answerable for what we learn how to see.”59 This is the exact epistemic complement to Mowles’s critique of the managerial “view from nowhere” (Part V). The four-box matrix is a god-trick in diagram form: it floats above the room, sorting situations into objective cells as though the sorter had no position, no stake, no power. The agreement axis is what breaks the trick. To ask where a group stands on agreement is to ask a situated question — whose agreement, reached how, under whose influence — and so to make the map answerable, in Haraway’s precise sense, for the field of power it is drawn within. A map that has deleted the agreement axis is a map that has refused to be answerable.

The deepest debt of the companion text is to Chris Rodgers, whose pragmatic dissent gives the rule for holding a map without being held by it: “it is the conversations that flow from the use of the frameworks that are important, not the frameworks themselves.”54 A framework is an occasion for talk, not a description of reality, and its falseness begins the instant it hardens into false concreteness. Beneath this lies the paradox Rodgers takes from Stacey and refuses to dissolve — that “human thought has always been paradoxical acts of immersing and abstracting at the same time,” so that “we have no option but to muddle through the practical implications of the immersion–abstraction paradox.”10 Here the companion essay and this study reach the same indictment by different roads. To abstract (to map) and to immerse (to act inside the situation) are not successive stages but simultaneous, paradoxical demands; the original diagram, as this Part argued, was built to hold that paradox open; the four-box mutation collapses it, offering an abstraction so concrete that immersion seems unnecessary — one need only read the box. Mowles states the standing condition that makes the collapse a falsehood: “we cannot stand outside our commitments, although we only fully realise what they are through articulating them and encountering others’ difference.”45 No one reads the box from outside; there is no outside.

What the companion offers in place of the frozen map it calls the living map: “a map drawn in the act of traversal, by a practitioner who arrives with orientation rather than a script, who treats every framework as an offer rather than an answer… and who holds the immersion–abstraction paradox open rather than collapsing it.”54 The word “offer” is Keith Johnstone’s, from the discipline of improvisation: “an offer is anything an actor says or does,” and the improviser “has to be like a man walking backwards—he sees where he has been, but pays no attention to the future.”60 A framework held as an offer is provisional, answerable, alive; held as an answer it is the dead map of Carroll’s fable, a duplicate that has ceased to do any work. This is, in the end, the same recovery this study presses from inside the diagram: the Stacey Matrix read as a living map is the five-zone original with its agreement axis intact and its central paradox open; read as a dead map it is the four-box graphic — and the distance between them is the distance between a map one travels by and a map one is trapped inside.

A framework held as an offer stays alive. Held as an answer, it becomes the map that ate the territory.
◆
II The Sources · Part II

The Two Primary Documents

"Stacey's matrix honors what we already have learned but also urges us to move with more confidence into some of the areas which we understand intuitively but are hesitant to apply because they do not appear as 'solid.'" — Brenda Zimmerman, NCCMT aide, 2001

The diagram reached the world not through Stacey's dense textbooks but through two intermediary documents, and to understand the reception one must read them closely. The first, Brenda Zimmerman's teaching aide, is the most faithful secondary treatment in existence; the second, the Praxis Framework entry, is the most influential transposition into project management. Together they mark the moment the diagram passed from theory into practice — and the first small surrenders that the later mutations would complete.

A. The Zimmerman Aide (NCCMT)

Brenda Zimmerman (1956–2014), professor at the Schulich School of Business, York University, produced in 2001 a teaching aide titled simply Ralph Stacey's Agreement & Certainty Matrix, later hosted by Canada's National Collaborating Centre for Methods and Tools.3 It is the single most responsible adaptation the diagram ever received, and it deserves close reading because almost every later "labelled" version descends from it — usually without acknowledgement and always with loss.

The fidelity of the reading

Zimmerman preserves both axes and all five zones, and her glosses on the axes are exact. On certainty: "Issues or decisions are close to certainty when cause and effect linkages can be determined… one can then extrapolate from past experience." On the far pole: "These situations are often unique… extrapolating from past experience is not a good method to predict outcomes." On the vertical axis she is equally careful — it "measures the level of agreement about an issue or decision within the group, team or organization," and "the management or leadership function varies depending on the level of agreement."3 She then walks each zone in turn, and her treatment of the political zone is the one that matters most for this study, because she states its logic with a clarity Stacey's own prose rarely achieved:

Some issues have a great deal of certainty about how outcomes are created but high levels of disagreement about which outcomes are desirable. Neither plans nor shared mission are likely to work in this context. Instead, politics become more important. Coalition building, negotiation, and compromise are used to create the organization's agenda and direction. Zimmerman, NCCMT aide, Zone 2

This is the diagram's sociological heart, preserved intact: where the facts are agreed but the values are not, no amount of analysis will help, because the problem is not epistemic but political. Part III develops this; Part IV gives it a thermodynamic reading.

The "deliberate edge" move

Zimmerman also adds something genuinely new to Stacey, and it is a real theoretical contribution rather than a dilution. Among the contexts for using the matrix she lists: "When innovations and creative alternatives are needed, this matrix can be used to deliberately try to increase the uncertainty and disagreement to nudge the system to the edge of chaos."3 This converts the diagram from a passive diagnostic into an active navigation instrument: a leader may choose to raise disagreement — to inject social entropy, in the language of Part IV — in order to push a frozen system into the generative far-from-equilibrium zone. No reading of the matrix as a mere sorting grid can accommodate this move, and it is the strongest evidence that Zimmerman understood the diagram as a dynamic field rather than a static typology.

The board-and-staff story

The aide's most revealing passage is an extended worked example that practitioners almost never cite. Zimmerman and a co-researcher studied a non-profit board over a full year and grew concerned that the board "seemed to complexify some very simple issues and drop some of the more challenging and fundamental issues," while the frustrated staff "began to over-simplify issues."3 At a retreat they had participants sort a year's decisions into simple, complicated and complex — and found "almost no disagreement about the issues and how they should be sorted." The laughter came when participants mapped the approaches they had actually used and "became aware of how they had used simple approaches for complex problems and vice versa." Two findings of this story are crucial and almost always lost in the diagram's popular use:

What the story showsWhy it matters
The matrix "did not represent a rigid landscape; rather… issues moved from one zone to another in part due to external conditions and in part due to the participants' perspectives"The diagram is explicitly dynamic and observer-relative — positions are not fixed properties but move with conditions and viewpoint. The four-box reading freezes exactly what Zimmerman said was fluid.
The value lay in "opening up discussion" and surfacing that board and staff were each mis-matching approach to zoneThe matrix's use is reflexive and conversational — a prompt for a group to examine its own taken-for-granted habits, not an instrument that delivers a verdict from outside.

Zimmerman’s own simplification — and the bridge it built

The same Zimmerman whose teaching aide preserved all five zones intact also produced the diagram’s first influential simplification — and it is this, far more than Stacey’s original, that the popular versions descend from. Taking the matrix from Stacey’s Complexity and Creativity in Organizations (1996), she overlaid on the agreement–certainty plane a graded vocabulary Stacey had not used as labels: simple, complicated, complex, anarchy. Where Stacey named functional zones — rational, political, vision — Zimmerman named degrees of difficulty. In her overlay (set out in Edgeware, 1998), the four categories form a continuum radiating from the close-to-certainty, close-to-agreement corner: simple at the rational corner (“follow the recipe”), complicated in the band beyond it (expertise and analysis), then the large central territory of the complex (emergence, trial, adaptation), and anarchy at the far corner.58 The reconstruction below makes the decisive feature unmistakable: complexity occupies the largest area of the whole field — the visual claim that most of organisational life is neither simple nor merely complicated.

Zimmerman’s move was decisive in two opposite directions at once. It kept faith with the original — both axes remain, and the categories are explicitly a continuum of “different mixes of agreement and certainty,” not a set of boxes — while introducing the very terms that would later be cut loose from it. The agile four-box of Part VIII is precisely Zimmerman’s overlay put through two further deformations: the two zones of “complicated” that flank the diagonal are merged into one, and the axes are renamed away from agreement and certainty altogether (Schwaber’s “requirements” and “technology”). In the morphology of the diagram — the transformation grids traced in Part VII — Zimmerman’s variant is the pivotal intermediate form: the deformation that relabelled the morphospace while preserving it, after which a second deformation could quietly collapse it. To read the genealogy is to watch the agreement axis survive Zimmerman, and die in the mutation that came next.

The Sorting Gaze — Simple, Complicated, Complex, and Who Decides Which

Those three words — simple, complicated, complex — are Zimmerman’s most travelled bequest, and in their most famous statement they arrived with three pictures. With Sholom Glouberman, in a 2002 paper on the reform of Canadian medicare, she gave the distinction the images that would outrun every diagram carrying it: simple is following a recipe to bake a cake — the recipe is essential, tested for easy replication, and “no particular expertise is required”; complicated is sending a rocket to the moon — formulae are critical but not sufficient, high expertise and coordination are needed, yet “sending one rocket increases assurance that the next will be OK,” for rockets resemble one another; complex is raising a child — “formulae have a limited application,” one child gives experience but “no assurance of success with the next,” expertise is neither necessary nor sufficient, and the outcome stays irreducibly uncertain because every child is unique.73 It is a genuinely useful distinction — and the very vocabulary it carries, Edgeware had already laid directly onto Stacey’s field.58 And read in the terms of this study it is a gaze — the moment one sees that, its politics become unavoidable.

For the operative act in the scheme is not solving the problem but classifying it: “determining the nature and perceived scale of a problem,” in the authors’ words, is the move on which everything afterward turns.73 And classification is exactly the demon’s act of Part VII — the informed look that drops a thing into a bin — and exactly Hacking’s making-up of kinds, the naming that the named then loop back to fit. Decide a problem is “complicated” and you summon experts, formulae, coordination, and the confident expectation that the last solution will transfer to the next; decide it is “complex” and you summon participation, adaptation, humility before the unique case. The classification does not describe the response; it dictates it — and through the response it shapes the outcome. The kind precedes the case, as the map precedes the territory (Part VII), and the hand that assigns the kind has already decided, before any work is done, what the problem will be permitted to be.

Glouberman and Zimmerman supply the decisive example themselves, and it is political to its roots. The overuse of emergency rooms, they observe, “is most often treated as a complicated problem” — a matter of throughput, economics, a narrow set of chosen variables — and so treated, “every iteration seems to worsen the situation”: resources are drawn to the emergency rooms from less urgent services, and the cycle tightens.73 Classed instead as complex, the same overuse shows itself a self-reinforcing system with a history. The problem did not change; the gaze did — and the gaze that called it “complicated” did not merely misread it but, by misreading, “shift[ed] the problem from one sector to another without solving it.”73 That displacement is the political face of the entropy export named in Part VII: the disorder a misclassification refuses to engage does not vanish; it migrates to where the chart is not looking. To choose the kind is to choose where the cost will fall.

The authors knew why the wrong classification is the tempting one, and gave it an image that is itself a parable of the gaze: “the sophistication of our models, theories and language for complicated problems can be as seductive as the lamplight.”73 It is the old joke of the drunkard hunting for his keys under the streetlight, not because he dropped them there but because the light is better. The complicated lens offers clarity, expertise, repeatability — better light — and so the managerial eye drifts toward it and away from the dark, complex ground where the problem actually lies. A gaze looks where it can see, not where it must; and a classification scheme that rewards the well-lit category trains a whole profession to keep its eyes on the lamppost.

And here is the cruellest turn in the genealogy. The very paper that minted “simple / complicated / complex” also set down, at length, the tensions that tear the health system — professional versus lay knowledge, evidence versus experience, rational planning versus self-organisation, competition versus collaboration, centralisation versus decentralisation, institution versus community.73 That catalogue is the agreement axis: the register of who disagrees with whom, and over what. The vocabulary, in other words, was born with its politics attached — coined inside an explicit map of disagreement. But, as this Part has shown, the words proved more transmissible than the structure that held them; they travelled onward stripped of the tensions, stripped of the axes, into the agile four-box where “complex” became merely a bin on a grid with no coordinate for agreement at all. The trichotomy that began as a tool for seeing the field’s disagreements was refashioned into a device for not seeing them — the Landauer erasure of Part VII performed, exquisitely, upon its own authors’ politics.

Crux · Classification Is the First Political Act

To sort a situation into “simple,” “complicated” or “complex” feels like description; it is decision. The label chosen summons its own response — recipe, expert, or participation — and through that response shapes what the situation becomes, so that the classifier’s gaze half-creates the kind it claims merely to have recognised. This is why the deletion of the agreement axis matters so acutely here: it leaves the sorter the power to assign kinds while erasing the dimension on which anyone might contest the assignment. A field with its agreement axis intact can still ask the only question that finally matters — who decided this was complicated rather than complex, and whose cost will that decision become? A field without it can only receive the verdict, and pay.

Larned’s diagonal, recovered just below, is the trichotomy’s continuous cousin: where Glouberman and Zimmerman cut the gradient into three named kinds, Larned drew it as one unbroken line of seven shading interventions — and the difference matters, for a continuum resists being turned into bins, while three discrete kinds positively invite the sorting the four-box would consummate. Both encode the same truth, the one the demon and the diagonal both teach: that “simple,” “complicated” and “complex” are not three sorts of problem lying in the world to be discovered, but three distances from equilibrium — three different relationships between an observer and a field — and that to name the distance is already to have taken a position within it. As Irene Ng distilled the consequence, “we can specify complicated systems; we can only intervene in complex ones.” The recipe and the rocket can be watched from outside; the child cannot. And the gaze that forgets the difference — that brings the recipe to the child — does not simplify the complex. It merely exports the complexity it refused to see, onto the child.

The Larned diagonal — recovered in full

The aide closes with the single most faithful adaptation the diagram ever received, and one almost entirely absent from the popular literature: the diagonal devised by Dr Stephen Larned, Vice President of Medical Affairs at Maine Medical Center. Rather than sorting issues into boxes, Larned drew a diagonal line through the field and placed seven distinct management interventions along it, each fitted to the degree of certainty and agreement at that point, with "the role of the leader shift[ing] quite dramatically as you move along the diagonal."3 It preserves the continuity that every four-box version destroys. Because it is so rarely reproduced, the full sequence is catalogued here:

#InterventionWhat the leader doesExample (Larned)
1DirectHighly structured and directive; does not anticipate emergent outcome (the rational corner)Asking an employee to arrange a luncheon or clean the warehouse
2Change work processesModify processes to facilitate self-organisation; planned change with intended or unintended emergenceA forklift firm connecting all service staff by CompuServe; their e-mail digest became the new service manual
3Modify structureAlter diversity, information and connections, less focused, hoping for beneficial emergenceChanging workgroups, meeting structures and information flow at Maine Medical Center
4Convene & interveneBring representatives of complex adaptive systems together, then a planned "intervention" to move to a new attractor (per Gareth Morgan)The Louisville integrated "Oncology Program" problem; a hospice-program problem
5ConveneBring systems together without direct control; act limited to convening in hope interaction will emergeAllopathic / osteopathic medical education (MMC / UNE)
6Examine, describe patternsObserve interactions too large or removed to affect; the task is to understandThe economy or the stock market
7Seek patternsScan "chaotic and disorganised" systems for emerging order; assume the object approaches high-dimensional chaosYouth, health, markets, life forms, weather, "global thermal drift"

Larned's seven interventions, arrayed on a diagonal from the rational corner to the edge of chaos — the diagram's most faithful adaptation, preserved in the Zimmerman aide.

Walking the Diagonal — The Seven Interventions, and the Eighth Condition

Catalogued in a table, the seven interventions look like a menu. They are nothing of the kind. They are a gradient — a single continuous line along which one quantity falls, steadily, from one end to the other: the amount of the world the leader’s gaze is permitted to command. Read in the terms this study has built, Larned’s diagonal is the matrix’s own theory of the gaze, drawn as a slope. At the rational corner the observer stands outside the system and sorts it, exactly as Maxwell’s demon sorts molecules (Part VII); at the far end the observer cannot touch the system at all, and may only watch it approach chaos. Every step up the line is a step in which the gaze surrenders a little more of the fantasy of standing outside — and a little more sovereignty passes from the leader to the field. To walk the diagonal is to learn, intervention by intervention, the lesson the four-box deletes: that where certainty and agreement are low, looking is no longer commanding.

1 · Direct. At the foot of the line, in the rational corner of high certainty and high agreement, the leader simply directs: highly structured, frankly authoritative, expecting no emergence — “asking an employee to arrange a luncheon or clean the warehouse.”3 This is the demon in its purest form: an observer who sees the field clearly, sorts it without resistance, and produces order by fiat. It works — but only here, where the molecules are slow and agreed. It is the single rung on which the managerial gaze actually holds the sovereignty the four-box silently assumes it holds everywhere. Thermodynamically it is the cheapest order there is, because the field already sits near equilibrium; almost no work is needed to keep it sorted.

2 · Change work processes. One step up, certainty has slipped, and the leader can no longer dictate the outcome — only the conditions. The intervention shifts from commanding results to redesigning processes “to facilitate self-organisation,” planned change that anticipates an emergence it cannot specify.3 Larned’s example is precise and lovely: a forklift firm wired its scattered service engineers together over CompuServe, and the e-mail digest they spontaneously produced became the new service manual — an artefact no manager wrote, generated by the connections a manager merely enabled. Here the gaze stops sorting molecules and starts altering the vessel; it acts on the system’s architecture and waits to see what the system makes of it.

3 · Modify structure. Higher still, the intervention grows “less focused” again: the leader alters the system’s diversity, its information, and its connections — workgroups, meeting structures, information flow — “hoping for beneficial emergence” without being able to name it in advance.3 Those three levers — difference, information, connection — are precisely the parameters on which a dissipative structure depends (Part VII): tune them and the system may fall spontaneously into a new ordered regime, or may not. The gaze is now a gardener’s, adjusting soil and light, having given up on designing the plant.

4 · Convene and intervene. At the middle of the line the leader can no longer act on the system from without at all; what remains is to bring its parts into one room — representatives of several complex adaptive systems — and then attempt a single planned “intervention” designed, in Gareth Morgan’s phrase, to move the whole toward a new attractor.3 (Larned’s cases: an integrated oncology programme, a hospice problem.) This is the highest rung on which intentional steering survives, and even here it has shrunk to a single aim pitched at the level of the basin, never the trajectory: the leader may hope to tip the system toward a different valley, but cannot choose the path it takes down.

5 · Convene. Strip away even the planned nudge and only the convening is left: the leader “brings systems together without direct control,” the act “limited to convening in hope interaction will emerge.”3 His example — bringing allopathic and osteopathic medical education into contact — is the gaze as host rather than sorter: it sets a table and withdraws. The leader’s whole contribution is now the occasion; whatever happens on the occasion belongs to the participants.

6 · Examine and describe patterns. Near the top, intentional action vanishes entirely. The system is “too large or too removed” to affect — the economy, the stock market — and the task collapses to pure understanding: observe the interactions; the task is only to grasp them.3 This is the rung at which Larned’s diagonal meets the heart of this study, for here the gaze has been stripped of every lever and reduced to what it always secretly was: a perspective. And the politics of perspective bites hardest exactly where leverage is least, because to describe a pattern is already to choose one — to draw a distinction and stand on its near side (Overture). The observer who can change nothing can still, by the way they name what they see, decide what everyone else takes the system to be.

7 · Seek patterns. At the very top the system is “chaotic and disorganised,” assumed to approach high-dimensional chaos, and even understanding turns provisional: the leader can only scan for an order that might be emerging — in “youth, health, markets, life forms, weather,” in what Larned strikingly calls “global thermal drift.”3 The gaze here neither sorts nor steers nor even confidently describes; it searches for a form it has no power to impose, in a field that may not yet possess one. It is the eye on the veranda at dusk, when the stones are nearly lost.

The eighth condition. And then the line stops — pointing, but never arriving, at the place every version of the diagram marks and none can manage: chaos itself. The seven interventions run toward an eighth state that is not an intervention at all but the limit where intervention ends — the far corner of maximum uncertainty and maximum disagreement which, read thermodynamically, is the state of maximum social entropy, where no sorting remains possible because every distinction has dissolved (Part VII). It is the diagram’s own fifteenth stone: the position the diagonal forever approaches and never occupies, the totality no walk completes. Larned was wise to draw an arrow there rather than a box. You cannot stand at chaos; you can only be drawn toward it as your certainty and your agreement give out beneath you.

Crux · The Diagonal as a Theory of the Gaze

Larned’s single line does, quietly, what the whole of this study argues at length. It says the leader’s gaze is sovereign only in one small corner, and that its sovereignty decays continuously as certainty and agreement fall — from directing (the demon sorting a field it stands outside), through convening (the host who merely sets the table), to seeking patterns (the eye that searches for an order it cannot impose), and at last to the chaos it may approach but never command. The four-box destroys exactly this. By deleting the diagonal’s continuity it deletes the education in the limits of looking, and leaves the manager with a graphic that silently promises the sovereignty of rung one across the entire field. To recover the diagonal is to recover the knowledge that, almost everywhere on the matrix, to look is not yet to control — and that pretending otherwise is the first political act.

Larned's own gloss on the value of his adaptation is, in miniature, the whole ethic the later mutations betray: it "demonstrates the value of 'owning' your own models," and shows "specific and concrete examples of how his management and leadership style need to vary by context."3 The diagonal is not a brand to be transmitted but a personal instrument, re-made by its user for his own situation — the opposite of the franchised four-box graphic.

The Verdict on Zimmerman

The Zimmerman aide is the model of how to extend a framework without breaking it: both axes preserved, all five zones preserved, continuity preserved (the Larned diagonal), the dynamic and observer-relative character made explicit, and one genuine theoretical addition (deliberate entry into complexity). What it bequeathed downstream, fatefully, was a vocabulary — simple / complicated / complex — that proved more transmissible than the structure it belonged to, and which travelled onward, in the agile mutation, stripped of the axes that gave it meaning.

B. The Praxis Framework Entry

The second great transmission was into project management, and its most careful expression is the Praxis Framework's library entry, which cites Stacey's third edition (2002).4 Praxis is notable for two things: it is unusually faithful to the five-zone original, and it is candid that the transposition is an extension beyond the diagram's design — "it was not specifically designed to address project delivery but is just as relevant to that area as any other."

What Praxis preserves

Praxis keeps all five zones and reads each as a governance prescription — what management style and control regime fits each region. Its rendering of the political zone is again exact and worth marking, because it survives here where the agile versions destroy it: "Some projects are very certain about which objectives are possible and how they can be delivered but there is less agreement about which objectives are of greatest value… skills such as negotiation, reaching a compromise and developing coalitions are important. Decision making becomes political rather than technical."4 The phrase "political rather than technical" is the diagram's sociological core, intact in a project-management standard a quarter-century after Stacey drew it.

Where Praxis begins to surrender

Yet Praxis also shows the first movements of the reduction the agile world would complete. It collapses Stacey's two distinct "complicated" edges (the vision zone and the political zone) toward a single "zone of complexity" framing, and — tellingly — its final move is to map the five zones onto a four-domain external scheme, noting that "Zone 1 is arguably the same as a Type A project in the WHOW model" and aligning the matrix with adjacent four-box frameworks.4 This is the characteristic gesture of the diagram's middle life: the five-zone gradient is quietly squared up against a four-cell typology, and the fit is declared "intuitive." Praxis performs the reduction gently and with caveats; the agile literature performs it without either.

FeatureZimmerman aide (2001)Praxis entry (2002 source)
AxesAgreement × Certainty, glossed exactlyAgreement × Certainty, glossed exactly
ZonesFive, walked individually; political zone explicitFive, read as governance regimes; political zone explicit ("political rather than technical")
DynamicsExplicitly fluid and observer-relative (board story)Implicitly static; positions read for method-selection
ContinuityPreserved (Larned diagonal)Not present; zones treated discretely
Reductive pressureResisted; the matrix is a conversationBegins; five zones mapped onto four-box external schemes (WHOW)
Synthesis · The Transmission Layer

These two documents together form the diagram's transmission layer — the membrane through which a theoretical artefact passed into the bloodstream of practice, acquiring on the way the labels (best practice, dialogue and debate, emergent practice) that would later float free of the axes entirely. Zimmerman's aide is the last faithful copy; the Praxis entry is the first managed one — the point at which the diagram begins to be governed by the needs of the field adopting it rather than the theory that produced it.

◆
III The Politics, I · Part III

The Political Axis, I — Disagreement as Irreducible

"Decision making becomes political rather than technical." — Praxis Framework, on Zone 2

Both primary documents preserve the vertical axis with care; almost every later version destroys it. To understand why this matters — and to prepare the long political argument of Parts III to VI and the thermodynamic argument of Part VII — we must see exactly what the agreement axis encodes, and why its deletion is the single most consequential event in the diagram's decline.

Two Axes, Two Kinds of Problem

The diagram's foundational claim is that organisational difficulty comes in two irreducibly different kinds, measured on its two axes. The horizontal axis names an epistemic problem: can we know what causes what? Its remedy, where remedy exists, is knowledge — analysis, expertise, modelling, experiment. The vertical axis names a political problem: do the people who must act agree on what is worth doing? And here is the decisive point — the political problem has no epistemic remedy. No quantity of analysis dissolves a genuine conflict of values, because the parties do not disagree about the facts; they disagree about which ends are desirable. To treat a political problem as though it were epistemic — to answer disagreement with more data — is one of the most common and most expensive errors in organisational life, and the diagram was built, in part, to name it.

This is why the political zone (high certainty, low agreement) is the diagram's most sophisticated region. It is the zone in which everyone knows exactly how to achieve each of several outcomes and they cannot agree on which outcome to pursue. Stacey's prescription — coalition, negotiation, compromise, the management of power — is not a failure of rationality but its appropriate form under conditions where rationality about means cannot settle a conflict about ends. The diagram, in this zone, is doing political theory: it recognises the organisation as an association of interests, not merely a problem-solving machine.

The Organisation as a Coalition

That recognition has a long and precise pedigree in organisational theory, which the diagram's later users almost never invoke. In the behavioural theory of the firm, Richard Cyert and James March argued in 1963 that an organisation has no single, unified set of goals; it is a coalition of individuals and groups with divergent interests, and its goals are the unstable product of continuous internal bargaining and "side payments."5 James Thompson, in Organizations in Action (1967), sharpened this into the concept of the dominant coalition — the shifting alliance of those whose combined power is sufficient, at a given moment, to set the organisation's direction.6 Three features of this tradition map exactly onto the agreement axis and deserve emphasis:

Dominant-coalition theoryImplication for the agreement axis
Organisational goals are negotiated among a coalition, not given (Cyert & March, 1963)"Agreement" is an achievement of bargaining, never a baseline; the axis measures the current state of a negotiation, not a fact
Coalition stability depends on members' continued satisfaction with the distribution of benefits (Murnighan & Brass)Agreement is contingent and reversible — it dissolves the moment members judge the coalition no longer serves their interests
Thompson: the more uncertainty (technology, environment), the more power-bases exist, and the larger the coalition grows6The two axes are coupled: rising uncertainty (rightward on the certainty axis) multiplies the parties whose agreement must be won, pushing the system downward on the agreement axis

That last coupling is worth dwelling on, because it is the diagram's hidden diagonal. Thompson's insight — that uncertainty breeds power-bases — means the two axes are not independent in practice: as a situation moves into genuine uncertainty, it tends also to move into disagreement, because where no one can know the right answer, more parties acquire a legitimate claim to a say. This is precisely why the large "zone of complexity" sits on the diagonal, far from both certainty and agreement at once: epistemic and political difficulty rise together, and the coalition swells exactly as the ground beneath it becomes unknowable.

What the Power Literature Establishes

The coupling is not a private inference; it is one of the firmest findings of the organisational scholarship on power, which Mowles surveys in Complexity: A Key Idea for Business and Society (2022). Drawing on Clegg and colleagues' comprehensive treatment and on Fleming and Spicer's review of more than fifty years of the field, he reports four cross-cutting themes, three of which bear directly on the agreement axis.45 First, there is broad consensus that power in organisations usually operates informally, even though most research fixes on its formal exercise — which is precisely why the agreement axis must be read in the shadow system (Part IV), not the org chart. Second, and decisively, there is the link between power and the management of uncertainty: most literature assumes organisations abhor uncertainty and that power is a way of alleviating it, but Fleming and Spicer note the converse power that "accrues to those organizational agents who can create uncertainty" about resources, promotion, or the criteria for decision. Mowles's gloss is the diagram's hidden diagonal stated outright: "the everyday emergence of uncertainty in organizations is a good place to start looking at the fluctuation of power between people."45 Movement rightward on the certainty axis is not politically neutral — it is itself a redistribution of power, and those who can manufacture uncertainty gain by it.

The third theme is a caution this study must heed. Much writing, Mowles notes, treats power "as though it operates in the abstract and impersonally, somehow 'through' people not by people" — a tendency he attributes partly to Foucault, who "writes extensively about the workings of power, but in a way that leaves the concept somehow without a human face."45 Against this he holds a deliberately paradoxical position: large-scale patterns (his example is "systemic racism") may seem to operate impersonally, yet "they nonetheless arise as a consequence of what human beings are doing in everyday situations." This is the Eliasian point exactly, and it guards the thermodynamic reading of Part VII from a real danger — that of treating "social entropy" as an impersonal force with no human face. The entropy of disagreement is always produced by people, in gossip and coalition and the daily negotiation of who counts; the physics is a description of what their interweaving produces, never a replacement for it.

The Eliasian Underwriting

Beneath the coalition lies a still deeper sociology, the one Stacey would build his mature work upon. Norbert Elias — whose figurational sociology grounds the theory of complex responsive processes that Stacey developed after abandoning the diagram — held that the irreducible unit of social analysis is the figuration: the web of interdependence binding people together, in which order arises from "the interweaving of the intentions and actions of many" and is the intention of none.7 On this account "agreement" is never a neutral, symmetric quantity that one could simply read off a population. It is a configuration of power: whose voice counts, whose dissent registers, whose preferences are treated as the natural state and whose as deviation — all of this is an outcome of the figuration, not an input to it. An organisation may register as "close to agreement" precisely because the voices that would carry disagreement have already been excluded, silenced, or never admitted.

Why the Agreement Axis Cannot Be Symmetric

The certainty axis is, at least in principle, symmetric: a fact is known or it is not, and two observers with the same evidence should converge. The agreement axis is structurally asymmetric, because it measures the distribution of power as much as the distribution of opinion. "High agreement" can mean genuine shared conviction or it can mean successful suppression of dissent; the axis alone cannot tell them apart. This asymmetry is exactly what the thermodynamic reading in Part VII will formalise — for the work required to produce agreement against the natural tendency of a diverse group to disperse into disagreement is a measurable cost, and that cost is borne by power.

The Deletion

The decisive corruption of the diagram, examined fully in Part VIII, consists precisely in the deletion of this axis. When Ken Schwaber, in 2004, renamed the vertical axis "Requirements Complexity," he replaced a political variable (do we agree on what is worth doing?) with a second technical one (are the requirements well-specified?).8 Both of Schwaber's axes now measured kinds of technical difficulty; the political dimension — and with it the entire recognition that organisations are associations of competing interests — simply vanished. As one analyst put it, "the interpersonal factor of interaction, which is decisive for Stacey… is completely lost; the diagram is reduced to a technocratic statement."9 The diagram that the agile world canonised is one from which politics has been surgically removed — which is to say, one from which the harder half of organisational reality has been removed. It is no accident that this politically-emptied version is the one that sells: a diagram without politics asks nothing uncomfortable of those who hold power.

◆
IV The Politics, II · Part IV

The Political Axis, II — Coalitions, Transcripts, Thought Collectives, and Symbolic Power

"If you want to see politics of power and claims for status you only need to visit Linked In on any day." — Chris Mowles, 2026

If the agreement axis is genuinely political, then it ought to illuminate the actual political life of organisations and professions — the coalitions, the gossip, the policing of orthodoxy, the public face and the private message. It does. This part follows the political axis out of the diagram and into the field, through four bodies of work that together compose a sociology of disagreement, and shows that the matrix's neglected vertical dimension is exactly the instrument that explains a phenomenon Chris Mowles anatomised in 2026: the politics of the professional tribe.

The Shadow System and Informal Coalitions

Stacey's own mature vocabulary already named the place where the politics of the agreement axis actually plays out. Against the legitimate system — the formal, designed organisation of roles and reporting lines — he set the shadow system: the informal network of relationships, conversations, alliances and gossip through which the real work of meaning-making and power is done. Chris Rodgers, a member of the Complexity Society writing directly in this tradition, made it the centre of his Informal Coalitions (2006), and the book repays close reading because it states, more plainly than Stacey ever did, the proposition this study turns on: that an organisation simply is its politics. Following Eccles and Nohria, Rodgers holds that "at all times, organizations consist of competing political coalitions of people" — and this, he says, "is the essence of the informal coalitions view of organizational dynamics. Coalitions form and re-form continually around different interests; each with the aim of defining aspects of the organization's agenda and shaping its outcomes."10 The agreement axis, read through Rodgers, is not a measure taken in the boardroom but a property of this shifting coalitional field: agreement is brokered informally, in corridors and direct messages, long before it is ratified formally — and disagreement, suppressed in the legitimate system, migrates into the shadow system as gossip.

Rodgers takes the term shadow side from Gerard Egan, for whom it names "those realities that often disrupt, and sometimes benefit, the business but are not dealt with in the formal settings of the organization."10 Egan's five components read almost as a gloss on the agreement axis itself: the messy informality that orthodox "rational" management cannot model; the idiosyncrasies of individuals that defeat universalist prescriptions; the organisation as a social system of in-groups and out-groups that "distort" the legitimate structure; the organisation as a political system in which "all organizations reflect a diverse range of viewpoints, motivations and self-interests, leading to competing coalitions of people, each seeking to define the organization's agenda"; and the cultural assumptions through which all of this becomes "known but undiscussable."10 That last word matters: drawing on Argyris, Rodgers notes that shadow-side issues are protected by "undiscussability" — and that "this undiscussability is itself often undiscussible," a double seal that keeps the real distribution of disagreement off every formal agenda. It is the organisational form of Scott's hidden transcript, and the reason a reading of "high agreement" is so often a reading of what has been rendered unspeakable.

Primary Source · Rodgers, Informal Coalitions (2006)

Rodgers's decisive move, for this study, is to state the diagram's hidden diagonal outright — the coupling of the two axes that Stacey left implicit. Cataloguing why political dynamics intensify, he writes that their "scale and scope… will be magnified the greater the level of uncertainty and change that exists, and the further away from agreement that people find themselves. The need for effective political leadership will also intensify in these circumstances."10 This is the agreement axis and the certainty axis named together as the two dimensions along which the demand for politics rises — written by a complexity-tradition author who, like Stacey, insists that "leaders are active participants not objective observers."

Crucially, Rodgers rejects the equation of politics with corruption. His functional politics holds that "organizational vitality and success depend on effective political processes," which "provide energy, enable differences to be dealt with effectively and organizationally beneficial results to be achieved in a complex and uncertain environment."10 Politics turns destructive only when it becomes "self-serving and manipulative," riding "roughshod over other people's interests"; approached ethically, "acting politically provides one of the keys for transforming ordinary performance into extra-ordinary performance." This is precisely Stacey's reading of the political zone — coalition, negotiation, compromise — recovered as a competence rather than a pathology, and it exposes the deeper cost of the agile mutation's deletion of the agreement axis: in erasing the political dimension, the four-box diagram erases not only a difficulty to be managed but a source of energy and renewal to be worked with.

Rodgers also anatomises the power that the agreement axis silently measures, distinguishing four interwoven forms: power that is instrumental (attributes directly used to modify others' knowledge, attitudes and behaviour); inner (the internal bases that let one wield instrumental power with more potency); relational (power that "exists, and can only be exercised, in relationship with other people"); and embedded (power lodged "in the structures, processes, language and other aspects of the organization that serve to define its formal rules and norms").10 The relational and embedded forms are the ones the diagram's vertical axis tacitly registers: "high agreement" may reflect relational persuasion freely given, or it may reflect power embedded so deeply in the rules and language of the organisation that dissent has become literally unsayable. Most managers, Rodgers observes, "associate power with command-and-control practices… and unsavory attempts to 'climb the greasy pole'" and so "sit on their hands" when asked whether they want it — even as "they all want to influence activities and outcomes." The agreement axis is the trace left by that disavowed, ever-present influence.

Finally, Rodgers supplies the mechanism by which agreement is actually made and unmade: conversation. Organisations, on his account, are "networks of self-organizing conversations," and culture is "not an object that can be designed and built by managers" but "the ongoing process of shared meaning making," existing "not so much 'outside' people as 'inside and between' them — within their internal dialogues and through the conversations that they have with each other."10 Interpretations "enter the folklore and mythology of the organization… these stories and myths help to embed the patterns of assumptions" that route all further sense-making "along established channels," and — the line that joins Rodgers to the whole argument of this study — "to the extent that an intervention helps to change the overall pattern of conversations within the organization, the organization itself will change, whether in the ways intended or otherwise." Agreement, in short, is a conversational achievement, perpetually re-made; the diagram's vertical axis is a snapshot of an unending negotiation conducted in talk.

Public and Hidden Transcripts

The most precise instrument for analysing this migration is the distinction the political scientist James C. Scott drew in Domination and the Arts of Resistance (1990) between the public transcript and the hidden transcript.11 The public transcript is the performance of the permissible — what parties are prepared to say openly, which "to a necessary degree cover[s] over our conflicts and discontents and pretend[s] harmony." The hidden transcript is what is shared only with trusted others: the jokes, the ribaldry, the "what we are really thinking" about the dominant. Scott's sharpest point, as Mowles emphasises, is a demythologising of "speaking truth to power," "given that power already knows the truth and is actually very comfortable with the hierarchy as it stands."11 The agreement axis, in Scott's terms, measures only the public transcript — the visible distribution of stated assent — while the real distribution of agreement and dissent lives in the hidden transcript, accessible only to those inside the relevant alliances. A reading of "high agreement" may register nothing but a well-maintained public transcript over a seething hidden one.

Thought Collectives and the Policing of Knowledge

The third body of work explains why disagreement is policed so fiercely even among people with no formal power over one another — among, for instance, the members of a profession or an intellectual school. The microbiologist and historian of science Ludwik Fleck, whose Genesis and Development of a Scientific Fact (1935) later inspired Thomas Kuhn, argued that knowledge is produced not by individuals but by thought collectives (Denkkollektive), which develop shared thought styles — habits, methods and idioms held in common — and which actively police "what does and doesn't count as acceptable knowledge or ways of thinking within the group and in contradistinction to other groups."12 Fleck identified, in Mowles's summary, five stages of active resistance to ideas that contradict the prevailing thought style: such ideas are first unthinkable (the style has permeated the very idiom — the word "system" for an organisation is Mowles's example); then unseen; then, if seen, kept secret; then explained away as an exception or as "something we have known all along"; and finally subjected to "a magical stage of theorising to find examples of where the status quo still prevails despite many countervailing examples."12

This is the political axis operating in the realm of ideas. A thought collective is a coalition whose currency is not budget or headcount but legitimacy — the authority to say what counts as knowledge.
