Its members maintain agreement (low internal entropy) by the continuous work of policing the boundary: certifying who is "really a member of the tribe," and denigrating rival collectives as deviant or derivative. Fleck's account joins directly to Elias's, for in The Established and the Outsiders (1965/1994) Elias and John Scotson showed how an established group sustains a "heroic we-identity" through praise gossip about itself and denigration gossip about those it excludes — and through "policing the established community to ensure that no member fraternises with the outsiders."13

In his Complexity: A Key Idea for Business and Society (2022), Mowles draws the thought collective tightly together with ideology and power, and the link is exactly the one this study is making about the agreement axis. Managerial "dominant ways of speaking" and the tools and techniques of organisational development, he argues, are "intended to clear up the messiness and the political jeopardy, the uncertainty of communicating with each other," and both are more or less ideological in the sense that they try to cover over existing power relations as though they are natural and to be taken for granted.45 They create a thought collective which, in Fleck's paradoxical sense, "both enables and constrains" — one must enter the thought style to do the work, "but at the same time it becomes difficult to realize that one is hemmed in by it." Mowles quotes Fleck directly:

Although the thought collective consists of individuals, it is not simply the aggregate sum of them. The individual within the collective is never, or hardly ever, conscious of the prevailing thought style, which almost always exerts an absolutely compulsive influence upon his thinking and with which it is not possible to be at variance. Ludwik Fleck (1935/1981: 41), quoted in Mowles, Complexity (2022)

The political consequence Mowles draws is precise and bears directly on the policing Part IV anatomises: "Anyone speaking out against the prevailing orthodoxy in contemporary organizations will similarly have found that the potential consequence of doing so is to risk exclusion from the group."45 Disagreement, in a thought collective, is not merely unwelcome; it is costly — it carries the risk of expulsion, which is why the work of holding a collective's agreement together is, in the most literal sense, the management of power. This is the agreement axis with its political and its thermodynamic faces fused: to hold a thought style against the dissent that would dissolve it is to do continuous work against an entropic drift, and the currency of that work is the threat of exclusion.

Cult Values and the Sleight of Hand

Douglas Griffin — Stacey's collaborator, whose argument against the diagram is examined in Part X — supplied the ethical anatomy of how this policing turns coercive. In The Emergence of Leadership (2002), Griffin distinguished systemic self-organisation from participative self-organisation, and argued that the systems view performs a "sleight of hand: affirming an all-encompassing whole," which then becomes the ground of "harmony and cult, caring and blaming."14 When a group treats its shared values as an all-encompassing whole to which every member must conform, those values become cult values: idealisations that brook no dissent, and whose violation is met not with argument but with blame and exclusion. The term is older than Griffin's use of it: Mowles traces it to G. H. Mead (1914, 1932), for whom cult values are "the values that we all have to recognize to remain a member of this particular group" — and Mowles adds the sharp political rider that "appealing to a cult value could also be a way of covering over less salubrious motives and actions."45 Griffin's point is that the appeal to a harmonious "whole" — the very move the four-box matrix makes when it implies agreement is the natural state — is the mechanism by which disagreement is converted from a legitimate political fact into a moral failing. The agreement axis, read through Griffin and Mead, carries an ethical warning: a group that cannot tolerate its position drifting down the axis will police the drift with cultic force, and will dress that policing as fidelity to a sacred whole.

Mowles on the Politics of the Tribe

These four strands — the shadow system, the hidden transcript, the thought collective, the cult value — are drawn together in Chris Mowles's 2026 essay "‘DM me if you want to know who I'm really talking about,'" which takes the politics of gossip as its subject and, without naming the diagram, supplies its richest contemporary application. Mowles opens with the accidental rupture of a hidden transcript — a senior clinician's contemptuous aside ("Of course X would have asked a stupid question like that") meant for one colleague but broadcast to a whole online meeting — and observes that gossip "is a way of both stabilising and/or undermining power relations," which "by and large… doesn't stray into public view, except by accident… or by design."15 Then comes the passage that names the phenomenon most exactly:

If you want to see politics of power and claims for status you only need to visit Linked In on any day. Members of LI who consider themselves authority figures in a thought collective may spend considerable time policing fellow members of that collective, pointing out where they stray from the orthodoxy, and who is, and isn't really a member of the tribe. Or they may critique other thought collectives for bringing nothing new that the former collective hasn't always known, or for being deviant. The authority figure might claim that they stand for a thought collective which is original and best. Gossip plays out in public and hidden transcripts and invitations to collude, turning the public, private: "DM me if you want to know who I really have in mind". Members of thought collectives will often point to the supposed cult-like and esoteric tendencies of other thought collectives, while covering over similar characteristics of their own. Chris Mowles, "‘DM me if you want to know who I'm really talking about,'" On Complexity, 14 May 2026

Every element of the political axis is present in this paragraph, and reading it through the matrix makes its logic explicit. The "authority figures" are the dominant coalition of a thought collective, their power consisting precisely in the legitimacy to police the boundary of acceptable knowledge (Fleck). "Pointing out where they stray from the orthodoxy, and who is, and isn't really a member of the tribe" is the maintenance of the established group's "heroic we-identity" through denigration gossip and the policing of fraternisation (Elias and Scotson). The claim "that they stand for a thought collective which is original and best" is the affirmation of an all-encompassing whole that converts shared values into cult values (Griffin). And the signature move — "DM me if you want to know who I really have in mind" — is the deliberate curation of the hidden transcript: an invitation to collude that "turns the public, private," recruiting a trusted other into an alliance while preserving deniability in the public transcript (Scott).

How the Stacey Matrix Explains the Politics of LinkedIn

The phenomenon Mowles describes is the agreement axis made visible in a professional field that has no certainty axis to fall back on. Among rival schools of management thought, no one can settle by experiment whose framework is "right"; the cause-and-effect linkages are unknowable, so the situation sits permanently at the far-right (far-from-certainty) edge of the diagram. By Thompson's coupling (Part III), that epistemic openness multiplies power-bases: where nothing can be proven, legitimacy is the only currency, and it is contested by every authority figure of every collective. The field therefore collapses onto the political zone — the work is entirely coalition-building, boundary-policing, denigration and alliance, because that is the only kind of work the conditions permit. LinkedIn is the political zone of the diagram rendered as a public arena: a domain of irreducible disagreement in which, exactly as Stacey said of that zone, "politics become more important — coalition building, negotiation, and compromise are used to create… direction." The diagram predicts that a field of high uncertainty and contested values will be governed by precisely the gossip-mediated politics of status that Mowles observes. Its imitators, having deleted the agreement axis, cannot see this at all; the original explains it exactly.

Mowles's closing generalisation is, in effect, a restatement of the agreement axis as the master variable of group life: online and face-to-face groups alike "function, one might even say depend upon, the push and pull of politics and power mediated through gossip," in which "so much is in play — claims for authority, trust, status, the power to interpret events and steer collective meaning-making, which is a negotiation of group membership."15 This is the vertical axis of the diagram, named in full: agreement is not a state but a perpetual negotiation of membership, conducted in transcripts public and hidden, and the "level of agreement" the diagram plots is the momentary score of an unceasing political contest.

Synthesis · The Agreement Axis in the World

The Mowles essay is the agreement axis walking around in the world. A thought collective is a coalition whose boundary-work is the social equivalent of a cell membrane — maintaining the distinction between inside and outside against the entropic tendency of distinctions to dissolve (the link forward to Part VII). The direct message is the hidden transcript's native medium: the private channel through which the high-entropy reality of disagreement is metabolised while the public transcript performs a low-entropy harmony. Policing, on this reading, is the work that holds a thought collective away from its own dissolution.

Classification Struggles and Symbolic Power — Who Classifies the Classifiers?

Beneath the particular politics this Part has traced — the coalitions, the transcripts, the policing of the tribe — runs a single general mechanism, and Pierre Bourdieu named it with more precision than anyone. For Bourdieu the deepest stake in social life is not the distribution of goods but the power to impose the principles of vision and division by which the social world is carved up and made to seem natural — and the supposedly neutral acts of describing, sorting and naming are nothing of the kind. The criteria used to build “objective” classifications, he insisted, are also “instruments — I should say weapons — and stakes in the struggle.”78 Every classification is a move in a classification struggle (la lutte des classements): a contest over whose vision of the field will be installed as the legitimate one. The Stacey Matrix is a classifying instrument, and on Bourdieu’s account it can never be the innocent diagnostic it presents itself as. To sort a situation into a zone is to raise a weapon in a struggle over what the organisation shall be taken to be.

Bourdieu called this kind of power symbolic power, and its exercise symbolic violence: the capacity to impose categories of perception that are accepted as legitimate by the very people they subordinate, so that domination proceeds through consent rather than coercion.78 Its supreme form is the power to name — for to name is to make a vision official, and Bourdieu held that the state itself is, in the last analysis, “the agency which possesses the power of legitimate naming.”78 The four-box is precisely such a naming machine. When it stamps a problem “complicated” rather than “complex,” or a field “simple” rather than contested, it does not record a fact; it exercises symbolic power — it imposes a principle of vision that summons the response the name foretells (the Sorting Gaze, Part II), and that the named then accept as simply the way things are. This is the political content beneath Hacking’s looping and Foucault’s examination (Part VII): the gaze that classifies is wielding symbolic power, and its classifications help make the world they claim only to read.

What makes symbolic violence violence, and yet keeps it invisible as such, is misrecognition — Bourdieu’s méconnaissance, the very word Lacan lent the mirror (Part X). Symbolic power works only on condition that its arbitrariness goes unseen: the imposed classification must be misrecognised as natural, technical, given — what Bourdieu called doxa, the order of things so taken for granted that it falls below the threshold of argument. The deletion of the agreement axis is the engineering of exactly this misrecognition. A matrix that keeps its agreement axis shows its classifications as contestable — as positions in a struggle over ends; a matrix that deletes it shows them as doxa, a neutral technical sorting with no visible stake and no visible loser, received by the classified as the plain shape of reality. The four-box does not win the classification struggle by argument. It wins by concealing that a struggle is taking place — the most complete form of symbolic violence there is, because it is the form not recognised as power at all.

And here Bourdieu turns the blade on the one who holds it, with the question that is the sharpest form of this whole study’s argument: who classifies the classifiers? The manager who reads the field and assigns its kinds imagines themselves outside it, in possession of a neutral overview — the posture Bourdieu mocked as scholastic or theoreticist epistemocentrism, the fantasy “that thought can transcend thought” and so escape the social conditions of its own seeing.79 Against it he set the discipline of reflexivity: the obligation to “objectify the objectifying subject,” to turn the classifying instruments back upon the classifier and so dispossess the knowing subject of “the privilege it normally grants itself.”79 There is no view from nowhere; the one who sorts is themselves positioned in the field, with interests, a history, a stake — the demon inside the box it sorts (Part VII), the eye that is itself seen (Part VI). The agreement axis, on this reading, is nothing less than the matrix’s own built-in reflexivity: it is the dimension on which the classifier becomes classifiable, on which one may ask of any verdict not merely “is this simple or complex?” but “who decided, from what position, in whose interest, and who will pay?” To delete the agreement axis is to exempt the classifier from classification — to install the manager in the unmarked, universal, view-from-nowhere position that Bourdieu spent a career refusing. To restore it is to subject the classifier, at last, to the same gaze they turn on everyone else.

Crux · The Axis That Classifies the Classifier

Bourdieu showed that to classify is to exercise power — a weapon in a struggle over whose vision of the world will count as legitimate — and that the power works only so long as it is misrecognised as neutral description. The four-box is symbolic violence of the purest kind: it imposes a principle of vision and division while erasing the one axis on which that imposition could be seen, contested, or charged to anyone. The deleted agreement axis was the line on which the classifier could themselves be classified — the matrix’s reflexivity, its standing answer to who classifies the classifiers? Cut it away and the manager ascends to the view from nowhere, sorting a field they pretend not to stand within. Restore it and the question returns, where it belongs, to the hand that holds the pen.

Group-Making, Social Magic, and the Classification That Classifies the Classifier

The reflexive turn has a constructive twin, and it is the deepest stratum of Bourdieu’s thought: classification does not merely register groups, it makes them. Loïc Wacquant, distilling the whole project, locates its centre in “the realization of categories” — the social alchemy by which a mental construct, alive at first only in individual minds, is turned into a concrete reality endowed with the power to act.80 A population existing merely as a “class on paper” is forged, through the performative work of naming and the rites that enforce it, into a class in fact.80 Read against the matrix, this dissolves the last of its innocence. The four-box does not chart pre-existing kinds of problem; it is an instrument for bringing them into being. Once an organisation adopts the diagram and acts upon it — routing the “complicated” to its experts, the “simple” to its best-practice manuals, the “complex” to its agile squads — the classification realises itself: the kinds become real because the organisation has begun to behave as though they were. This is the sociological engine beneath Hacking’s looping (Part VII). The diagram is a machine for manufacturing the very kinds it claims only to find.

Bourdieu’s formula for this double life is exact: social groups, he wrote, “exist twice” — in the first-order objectivity of material distributions, and in the second-order objectivity of the classifications and representations that agents impose upon them.80 The classification struggle is the contest over the second order; and once a representation prevails, it acts back upon the first, reshaping the reality it claimed only to describe. Classifying, on this account, cuts sharp official divisions into a world that comes only in gradients — it produces discontinuity out of continuity. Here lies the matrix’s most fundamental violence, and it is a violence done to a continuum. The certainty axis and the agreement axis are continua: in the world there are no joints between “simple” and “complicated,” only degrees of shading. The four-box makes discontinuous cuts — four discrete cells where there were two gradients — and presents the cuts as joints in nature. They are not found but instituted, in the strict sense of Bourdieu’s rites of institution.81 And the deletion of the agreement axis is the most radical cut of all: not the partition of a continuum but the collapse of one, a whole dimension of difference folded to a point. The morphospace of Part IX is exactly what such a collapse destroys.

Bourdieu reserved a particular warning for the part his own discipline plays in this work, and it falls on the Stacey Matrix with full weight. Social science, Wacquant observes, is itself drawn into group-making whenever its techniques and idioms are borrowed by the powerful to project “a falsely rationalized vision of… rule.”80 A management-science diagram — axes, quadrants, the whole apparatus of the figure — is precisely such a borrowed technique. The four-box’s scientism is not a neutral packaging of insight; it is itself a symbolic weapon, lending the manager’s interested cut the borrowed authority of science and so converting a partisan act of division into an objective-seeming finding. The very form — the look of a diagram, the air of a diagnostic instrument — is the misrecognition machine: it is what persuades the classified to receive an imposition as a measurement. The diagram does not merely carry symbolic power; its scientific costume is the symbolic power, worn where it cannot be seen.

And so to the maxim in which Bourdieu compressed the entire reflexive argument. In Distinction he wrote that “taste classifies, and it classifies the classifier.”81 Every act of classification is also, unavoidably, a self-portrait: in sorting the world one betrays one’s own position within it — one’s habitus, one’s interests, what one is equipped to perceive and constitutionally blind to. The verdict is never only about its object; it is always also a confession about its author. This is the last and most precise answer to who classifies the classifiers? — they classify themselves, in the very gesture of classifying others. When a manager pronounces a field “simple” or “complex,” the judgement quietly discloses the manager: the position from which such a field looks simple, the interest such a reading serves, the disagreement it is convenient not to see. The agreement axis is the dimension on which that disclosure becomes legible — the surface on which the classifier’s own face appears in the act of looking. To delete it is to airbrush the author out of the verdict (the crack retouched from the mirror, Part X); to restore it is to reinstate the maxim in full: every sorting of the field is also a sorting of the sorter.

Coda · Taste Classifies the Classifier

Bourdieu’s sociology turns the matrix inside out. The diagram does not find kinds of problem; through the social magic of naming it helps make them, turning a “class on paper” into a fact by getting an organisation to act on it. It does so by cutting a continuum into discrete official cells and dressing the cut in the costume of science — a falsely rationalised vision of rule. And the cut it makes most violently, the collapse of the agreement axis, is exactly the cut that hides its author — for taste classifies, and it classifies the classifier, and the agreement axis was the line on which the classifier’s own position could be read. Erase it and the manager sorts from nowhere; restore it and the sorting sorts the sorter.

◆
V The Philosophy · Part V

The Pragmatist Engine

"Thought, or ‘inquiry,' results from doubt… Resolution and unobstructed conduct are products of belief, which is a form of stability and satisfaction." — on Peirce

The political axis has a floor beneath it, and the floor is a theory of how human beings make meaning at all. Stacey's mature work rests on American pragmatism — Peirce, Mead, Dewey — and tracing the agreement axis down to this foundation reveals why disagreement is not a surface nuisance but a feature of the very process by which thought and organisation come into being. It also supplies, in Peirce's own terms, the bridge to the thermodynamics of Part VII.

Peirce: Doubt, Belief, and the Settling of Opinion

For Charles Sanders Peirce, thought begins not in curiosity but in doubt — "a state in which habitual actions are blocked or confused and from which organic irritation and irresolution result" — and aims at belief, which is "a form of stability and satisfaction."16 Inquiry is the struggle to pass from the irritation of doubt to the repose of settled belief. This is a quietly momentous framing for the agreement axis, because it makes agreement and belief structurally identical: both are states of stability and satisfaction reached by passing through, and out of, a prior unsettlement. Disagreement, on Peirce's account, is collective doubt — the blocked, irritated, unresolved condition from which the work of inquiry must move. And just as Peirce insisted that belief is not a private possession but is fixed in a community of inquiry through self-correcting method, so agreement in an organisation is fixed in a community through the political work of the shadow system. The agreement axis measures how far a community has travelled from the irritation of collective doubt toward the satisfaction of settled common belief.

Peirce's account of how genuinely new belief arises is equally pertinent. He named the logic of hypothesis-formation from a surprising fact abduction — "the first stage of inquiry," in which a fact that resists assimilation to existing categories forces the formation of a new one.17 Abduction is the cognitive correlate of Prigogine's "order through fluctuation" (Part VII): a perturbation that, instead of being smoothed away, is amplified into a new structure of understanding. The zone of complexity, where the rational toolkit fails and novelty must be created, is the zone of collective abduction — the place where surprising facts that the old order cannot digest force the emergence of new shared meaning.

Mead: The Living Present and the I/Me Dialogue

George Herbert Mead, who knew Peirce's work on signs and whom Dewey called one of the most fertile minds in American philosophy, supplied the social mechanism.18 For Mead, mind and meaning arise in the gesture-and-response of social interaction: meaning is located not in either party but in the social act itself, and a gesture that calls out in the gesturer the same response it calls out in the other is a significant symbol, through which a person becomes able to take the attitude of the other toward themselves. The self, for Mead, is an internal dialogue between the "I" (the spontaneous, responding subject) and the "me" (the self seen through the eyes of the generalised other). This is the micro-foundation of the agreement axis: to know whether one agrees is already to have taken the attitude of the group toward one's own position — agreement and disagreement are constituted in the same I/me dialogue through which the self is constituted. There is no agreeing subject prior to the social process; the subject and its agreements co-arise.

Mead's living present completes the picture and bears directly on why the diagram cannot be a static snapshot. For Mead the present is "thick" — the anticipated future acts back on the remembered past to alter its meaning, even as the reconstructed past reshapes the anticipated future. Agreement is therefore never simply held; it is perpetually reconstructed in each present interaction, which is exactly what Zimmerman observed when she reported that issues "moved from one zone to another… in part due to the participants' perspectives" (Part II). The agreement axis plots a position in a living present that is always already moving.

Dewey: Inquiry as the Entanglement of Means and Ends

John Dewey, who studied briefly with Peirce and brought Mead to Chicago, "dissolved the separation of thinking and doing through his theory of inquiry."19 For Dewey, inquiry begins in an indeterminate situation and works toward a warranted resolution, and crucially the means and ends of inquiry are entangled — the method is not neutral but constitutes the inquiry from its first moment. This is the pragmatist ground of the diagram's deepest claim: that one cannot resolve a political problem (a conflict over ends) with a technical method (an inquiry into means), because the choice of method is itself a political act that shapes which ends can emerge. To bring a four-box sorting grid to a value-conflict is, in Deweyan terms, to let a technical means silently determine a political end — to pretend the question of what is worth doing has been settled when it has only been hidden.

Rhetoric, Judgement, and the Ideology of the View From Nowhere

The pragmatist line surfaces, in contemporary form, in Mowles's argument for what he calls "dwelling in uncertain speech." Against the orthodox appeal to the abstract — "either the abstractions of quantification realized as metrics, targets or financialization or the idea that we can create procedures, rule-based abstractions for doing away with hierarchy, power and politics" — he argues frankly "in favour of partiality, prejudice, in the sense of inevitably bringing prejudgements and rhetoric in navigating uncertainty."45 The conversational work of organisations "involves a lot of persuading, coaxing and appealing to each other's judgement," and this is "a legitimate, even helpful way of recognizing each other in the midst of complexity." The claim is pragmatist to its root: reason is not a view from nowhere but arises "in social and specific contexts and works well to help people function in groups" (Mowles follows Gigerenzer and Mercier & Sperber here), so that deliberation about the particular situation is more use than "ideas taken from 'best practice' or aspiring to a view from nowhere."45

The sentence that fuses this with the agreement axis is Mowles's diagnosis of what the appeal to abstraction is doing: "power always manifests itself one way or another because we are interdependent. And the attempt to cover it over or wish it away with appeals to rationality, to quantification and to the 'vision' of the organization is also an ideological appeal."45 Here the three movements of this study close into one. The vision zone of the diagram (high agreement, low certainty) and the rational corner (the home of metrics and best practice) are exactly the registers Mowles names — and his point is that to retreat into either, in a situation that is actually political, is not to escape power but to disguise it. The deletion of the agreement axis is the cartographic form of this disguise: a diagram that offers metrics-and-best-practice in one corner and shared-vision in another, with the political zone erased, is a map drawn in the ideology of the view from nowhere. Rhetoric, persuasion, the open acknowledgement of partiality — the things the political zone demands — are not failures of rigour but the honest alternative to a false objectivity.

To erase the political axis was never to simplify the map. It was to disguise power as method.

Weick: Organizing as the Reduction of Equivocality

The pragmatist account of meaning-making found its most influential organisational expression in Karl Weick, whose The Social Psychology of Organizing (2nd ed., 1979) dissolved the noun "organisation" into the verb organizing, and in doing so supplied the missing hinge between the pragmatist engine of this Part and the thermodynamic reading of the next. Organizing, in Weick's deliberately exact definition, is "a consensually validated grammar for reducing equivocality by means of sensible interlocked behaviors. To organize is to assemble ongoing interdependent actions into sensible sequences that generate sensible outcomes."46 Two words in that sentence carry the whole connection to the agreement axis.

The first is equivocality. Organizing, Weick says, is "directed initially at any input that is not self-evident"; the occasions for "sizable collective activity" are "happenings that seem to have more than one meaning (they are equivocal)," and "once these inputs have become less equivocal, there is a decrease in the amount of collective activity directed at them."46 Equivocality is informational entropy in precisely the Shannon sense Part VII will formalise — a spread of probability across many possible meanings, a high-uncertainty distribution over interpretations — and organizing is the work of collapsing that distribution toward a shared reading. Weick had named organizing as entropy reduction, in all but the word, two decades before this study draws the thermodynamic moral; his "reducing equivocality" and the agreement axis read as −H are the same quantity described in two vocabularies.

The second word is agreement itself. Organizing, Weick insists, is "first of all grounded in agreements concerning what is real and illusory, a grounding that is called consensual validation" — Harry Stack Sullivan's term for "the things people agree upon because their… deeply common interpersonal experiences make them seem objectively so."46 This is the agreement axis given a constructivist floor: what an organisation treats as real is not handed to it but agreed, and the agreeing is the organizing. The elementary unit of that work is the double interact — one party acts, a second responds, the first adjusts — the circular, interlocking exchange (Weick takes it from Allport) that is the organisational cousin of Mead's gesture-and-response and the cell from which all larger agreement is assembled.

Weick's most radical move, enactment, closes the loop with the political and the thermodynamic alike. People in organisations, he argues, "create the environment that confronts them"; they "repeatedly impose that which they later claim imposes on them"; and therefore they "need to act to find out what they have done."46 The environment an organisation responds to is in part one it has talked into being — which is exactly why the agreement axis never records an external fact but a jointly enacted one, and why Mowles's thought collectives (Part IV) can police "reality" itself rather than merely opinions about it. Enactment is the constructivist engine beneath the whole vertical axis: the distribution over goal-states that the axis measures is not discovered in the world but enacted in the conversation, and the continuous, equivocality-lowering labour of narrowing it is what Weick called organizing and what this study, in its final vocabulary, calls the work of holding a system away from equilibrium.

“Any Old Map Will Do” — Enactment, and Why a False Chart Still Builds a World

Weick illustrated enactment with a parable he took from a poem of Miroslav Holub’s, and it is the deepest thing said anywhere in this study about what a diagram does. A detachment of soldiers is lost in the Alps in a blinding snowstorm; after days they give themselves up for dead — until one finds a map in his pocket. The map calms them; they make a plan, wait out the storm, descend, and live. Only safely back at camp does someone notice that the map was not of the Alps at all. It was a map of the Pyrenees.74 Weick’s moral is as exact as it is unsettling: “when you are lost, any map will do.”74 The map did not show the soldiers the way down — it could not, for it was the wrong map; it did something more powerful, ending their paralysis and animating them to act, and the acting, not the map’s accuracy, found the path. Sensemaking, Weick concluded, is “driven by plausibility rather than accuracy”; in it “facts are a nicety, not a necessity.”74 A map works not by being true but by being believed enough to move you.

This is why Weick liked to compress his whole theory into a single question: how can I know what I think until I see what I say?74 We do not first perceive a situation accurately and then act on it; we act, and then read our own action backward into a sense of what the situation must have been — sensemaking is retrospective, and the believing precedes the seeing it claims to follow. Lay this over enactment and the loop closes: a plausible map animates action; the action enacts an environment; and the enacted environment is then read back as the reality the map had merely “described.” The map breeds the territory (Part VII), and it does so without ever having needed to be correct.

Now the full danger of the four-box becomes visible, and it is graver than mere inaccuracy. The popular matrix is exactly such a map: plausible, confidence-giving, action-animating. And by Weick’s logic the fact that it is wrong about an organisation — that it deletes the politics, flattens the agreement axis, presents a contested field as a neutral technical one — is no objection to its working. It is the engine of its working. A map of the Pyrenees gets you down a mountain in the Alps; the four-box gets you a depoliticised organisation — neither because it is accurate, but because each animates a kind of action that enacts the world it depicts. Managers who believe a situation is a simple technical sort act accordingly; their action enacts a workforce, a set of conversations, a distribution of permitted speech that behaves as though politics were absent; and the resulting silence is then read back as proof that the map was right. The wrong map does not fail. It succeeds at building the wrong world.

What, then, would the missing agreement axis have given an organisation? Not a more accurate map — Weick has just taught us that accuracy is not the operative virtue — but the one thing the four-box withholds precisely by its absence: the capacity to notice that it is enacting rather than discovering, that it is descending the Alps on a chart of the Pyrenees. The agreement axis is the instrument of reflexivity; it is the line along which an organisation can see its own disagreement, and so recognise that the order it inhabits was made, by action, out of a plausible fiction — and might, by other action, be made otherwise. There is a last and fitting irony here, and it belongs to a study of the politics of representation. Weick’s parable about maps that need not be true was itself borrowed, almost verbatim and for years without attribution, from Holub’s poem, and its very moral is disputed in the literature.74 The story that taught a generation that accuracy is beside the point was itself transmitted inaccurately — which is either the deepest confirmation of its thesis or its sharpest refutation, and the agreement axis is precisely what one would need in order to tell the difference.

Why Pragmatism Grounds the Whole Argument

The pragmatist engine ties the three movements of this study together. Peirce makes agreement the collective form of belief — a state of stability and satisfaction won from the irritation of doubt, which is the psychological face of what Part VII will call a low-entropy state won from disorder. Mead makes agreement co-arise with the self in the living present, so that the axis is intrinsically dynamic and never a fixed property. Dewey makes the choice of method a constitutive political act, so that deleting the political axis (the agile mutation) is not a simplification but a covert determination of ends. And Weick makes organizing itself the reduction of equivocality — entropy lowered through interlocked talk — so that the agreement axis and the entropy axis are revealed as one quantity seen twice — while his enactment shows that such a map need not even be true to build the world it names. The agreement axis is not a measurement laid over an organisation from outside; it is a reading of where a community of inquiry has arrived in the perpetual, power-laden work of settling collective belief.

◆
VI The Psyche · Part VI

The Psychodynamic Dimension

"I watched as his face and ears burned red — physically he began to lean away from her in his seat as though responding to a blast." — Chris Mowles, on the moment a hidden transcript ruptured into the open

The politics of the agreement axis is not only cognitive and strategic; it is affective and unconscious. The tradition Stacey joined when he trained as a group analyst — the group analysis of S. H. Foulkes — supplies the missing psychodynamic dimension, and it explains both the charge that disagreement carries and the rupture by which a hidden transcript suddenly becomes public. It also prepares, through the old idea of catharsis, the thermodynamic reading to come.

The Group Unconscious and the Foulkesian Matrix

Stacey qualified as a group analyst at the Institute of Group Analysis in 1998 and ran clinical groups until about 2004 — in the very years he was abandoning the diagram and building complex responsive processes.20 The tradition he entered, founded by Foulkes, holds that the group is more than the sum of its members: unconscious processes are not merely individual but shared, acted out in what Foulkes called the group matrix, "the hypothetical web of communication and relationship in a given group" (developed fully in Part X).21 Disagreement, on this account, is rarely only about the stated issue. It carries the freight of transference, rivalry, envy, the struggle for the leader's regard, and the unconscious life of the group as a whole. This is why a position on the agreement axis can be so much more charged than its ostensible content warrants: what looks like a dispute about a decision is also a contest within the group's unconscious matrix, and the "level of agreement" is partly a register of unconscious group dynamics that no participant can fully see.

Catharsis and the Rupture of the Hidden Transcript

The concept that links the psychodynamic to the political and the thermodynamic is the oldest in the therapeutic vocabulary: catharsis — from the Greek katharsis, purgation — the discharge of accumulated and suppressed affect.22 In the psychoanalytic tradition from which group analysis descends, suppressed emotion is "strangulated affect," trapped energy that, denied a healthy outlet, accumulates until it is discharged, often suddenly. Catharsis is consistently identified as one of the principal therapeutic factors in group psychotherapy: "members of the group experience and then release strong emotions related to their problems."23

This is precisely the dynamic of Mowles's anecdotes, and reading them through catharsis reveals their structure. In the first, a contemptuous aside "brought something into the open which had been simmering below the surface" — an accumulated charge, long held in the hidden transcript, discharged by accident into the public one.15 In the second, more powerful, a member of staff who "had suffered more than most eventually became so frustrated that she spoke about what had happened to her" — a cathartic rupture in which strangulated affect, accumulated under months of bullying and silence, finally broke into speech in the presence of the very Director responsible, whose body "began to lean away from her… as though responding to a blast."15 The image is exact: the discharge of accumulated tension is a release of stored energy, felt by those present as a physical force. Catharsis, in other words, is the affective form of an entropic discharge — the sudden release of a gradient that has been held under pressure.

The Charge on the Agreement Axis

The psychodynamic dimension explains why movement on the agreement axis is so rarely smooth. Suppressed disagreement does not simply persist at a steady level; it accumulates, as strangulated affect, in the hidden transcript and the group unconscious — and accumulated charge discharges suddenly, cathartically, often at a moment and in a form no one chose. The agreement axis, read psychodynamically, is under tension: a public transcript of harmony maintained over a hidden transcript of grievance is a system storing energy, and the storage is not free. Part VII names this in thermodynamic terms; group analysis names it in affective ones. They are the same phenomenon seen from inside and outside — which is why Mowles's bullied employee, and Prigogine's far-from-equilibrium system, both release their stored gradient in a sudden, structure-changing event.

There is a further connection, which Mowles's own practice embodies. Group analysis works by making it safe enough for the hidden transcript to enter the public one — for the group's suppressed disagreements and affects to be spoken and contained rather than discharged destructively. The "containing function" of the analytic setting is what allows catharsis to be therapeutic rather than merely explosive. This is the practitioner's version of the diagram's deepest counsel: that disagreement in the zone of complexity must be worked with, held open and metabolised, not suppressed into a false harmony that will eventually rupture. The matrix, the shadow system, the hidden transcript and the group unconscious all describe the same truth from different angles — that the political and affective charge of disagreement is real, that it accumulates when suppressed, and that the work of organisation is in large part the work of holding and transforming it.

The Internalised Gaze — Self-Surveillance and the Privatising of Dissent

The group analyst’s hope is that disagreement can be spoken and held. But there is a darker fate, and it is the one a diagram with no coordinate for disagreement quietly arranges. To reach it we must follow the gaze of Part VII one step further — inward. Foucault’s panopticon does its disciplinary work, he showed, not by being manned but by being believed in: the inmate, never certain whether the central tower is occupied, comes to watch himself, and so “becomes the principle of his own subjection.”70 The examining gaze, once internalised, no longer needs an examiner. And the classifying gaze of the matrix (Parts II and VII) is internalised in just this way: the manager and the team learn to sort themselves, to pre-audit their own situation against the grid, to ask not “what do we want?” but “which box are we in, and am I performing it correctly?” The watcher on the veranda has moved inside the skull.

The most acute diagnosis of where this leads is Byung-Chul Han’s. The old disciplinary society ran on negativity — the should, the prohibition, the external overseer; the new achievement society runs on positivity — the can, the project, the limitless self — and its signal innovation is to dispense with the overseer altogether. “No coercion is required”; having internalised the imperative to perform, “we exploit ourselves,” and this auto-exploitation “is more efficient than allo-exploitation, for the feeling of freedom attends it. The exploiter is simultaneously the exploited.”75 The achievement-subject is at once master and slave, perpetrator and victim, and burns out not because it is driven but because it drives itself; burnout and depression, on this account, are not private misfortunes but “the direct pathologies of the achievement society.”75 And Han names, in one sentence, the precise fate this study has been tracking: “even class struggle has transformed into an inner struggle against oneself.”75

That sentence is the psychodynamic translation of the deleted agreement axis. Consider what becomes of disagreement in an organisation whose governing diagram has erased the dimension on which disagreement could appear. The conflict is not abolished — the second law forbids that (Part VII) — but it is denied a public coordinate; and a structural disagreement with no public coordinate does not merely wait in the shadow system. It is driven inward. The team member who finds herself persistently “out of alignment” has no axis on which to read her misalignment as a political position — a legitimate disagreement about ends — and so reads it, as the achievement society trains her to, as a personal failing: not yet optimised, not yet bought-in, not yet the team player the grid requires. The class struggle has become an inner struggle against herself. The strangulated affect this Part began with (Foulkes’s phrase) and the bound social free energy of Part VII (Bailey’s) are now lodged not in the group but in the individual psyche, accumulating there under the smiling injunction to align — until they discharge as the cathartic rupture described above, or, more often and more quietly, as the burnout Han describes: the achievement-subject collapsing under the weight of a consensus it was never given the standing to refuse.

This is why the affect that enforces the internalised gaze is so often shame rather than fear. Fear is the register of the external overseer; shame, as Sartre saw in the look that catches us at the keyhole, is the register of the gaze we have taken inside — “shame is shame of oneself before the Other,” even when the Other is now only the grid in one’s own head (Part VII).71 It is no accident that this Part opened with a body burning red and leaning away — the somatics of shame — nor that the rupture, when it finally came, took the form of a confession wrung from someone who “had suffered more than most.” The internalised gaze turns the carrier of an unrecorded disagreement into someone ashamed of carrying it; and shame, unlike spoken dissent, makes no claim on the organisation. It asks only that the sufferer fix herself.

Crux · The Cost That Falls Inside the Body

The deletion of the agreement axis can now be followed to its final resting place. It hides the disagreement from the chart (Part VII); it exports the disagreement to the shadow system (Parts IV, VII); and here, at the last, it interiorises the remainder — depositing the unrecorded conflict inside the body of whoever the gaze declined to record, where it is borne not as politics but as private inadequacy, anxiety and burnout. This is the most complete erasure a diagram can perform: it persuades the very people who carry its suppressed disagreement that what they carry is a personal defect rather than a political position. To restore the agreement axis is, among everything else this study has claimed for it, an act of psychic restitution — it gives the “misaligned” back the knowledge that their misalignment has a coordinate, a legitimacy, and a name that is not their own failure.

◆
VII The Physics · Part VII

The Thermodynamics of Disagreement

"Order and disorder are created simultaneously… the level of complexity rises as the generation of entropy, or dissipation, increases." — Ilya Prigogine

We come now to the centre of the study — the bridge the existing literature has only gestured toward. The claim is precise and, once seen, difficult to unsee: the diagram's vertical axis, the axis of agreement, is at root a measure of social entropy, and the whole field the diagram maps is a field of non-equilibrium thermodynamics. Disagreement is entropy; consensus is a low-entropy state bought by work; the maintenance of agreement is a thermodynamic cost borne by power; and the "zone of complexity" is a far-from-equilibrium dissipative regime — the one place where order can arise through fluctuation. This is not a loose metaphor. It rests on three rigorous bodies of theory, and it corrects a persistent inversion at the diagram's far corner.

The First Pillar: Prigogine and Dissipative Structures

Ilya Prigogine won the 1977 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for showing that order can arise spontaneously in systems held far from thermodynamic equilibrium.24 His dissipative structures — Bénard convection cells, the Belousov–Zhabotinsky reaction, and, he argued, living organisms and human societies — are open systems that maintain their internal order only by continuously importing energy and exporting entropy to their environment. Four conditions define them: openness (exchange with the environment), far-from-equilibrium operation (not at maximum entropy), non-linearity (feedback that amplifies fluctuations), and dissipation (the export of entropy).25 The governing relation is the entropy balance:

dS = diS + deS
The total entropy change of a system (dS) is the sum of entropy produced internally by irreversible processes (diS ≥ 0, always non-negative) and entropy exchanged with the environment (deS, which can be negative). A structure stays ordered — keeps diS from accumulating into disorder — only by running a sufficiently negative deS: by exporting entropy faster than it generates it.

Two features of Prigogine's theory matter decisively for the diagram. First, the deep and counter-intuitive result that order and entropy are created together: as Prigogine put it, "increase in entropy is not an increase in disorder, for order and disorder are created simultaneously," and "the level of complexity rises as the generation of entropy, or dissipation, increases."26 Complexity is not the absence of entropy production; it is a particular regime of high entropy production. Second, dissipative structures are sustained by "order through fluctuations" — far from equilibrium, "certain fluctuations, instead of regressing, may be amplified and invade the entire system," carrying it to a new and more complex state.27 Order does not arise here by suppressing variation but by amplifying it. This is the exact thermodynamic content of Stacey's "zone of complexity," and of Zimmerman's "deliberate edge" move, in which a leader injects disagreement precisely to push a frozen system toward generative instability.

In Plain Terms · A dissipative structure

A whirlpool, a candle flame, a living cell, a thunderstorm — an ordered pattern that exists only while energy flows through it. Cut the flow and it vanishes at once, melting back into formless equilibrium. It stores nothing; it is the flow, briefly holding a shape. Hold that picture: it is the best single image for what an aligned, creative organisation actually is — and for why it dies the moment the effort stops.

The Second Pillar: Bailey and Social Entropy

The bridge from physics to organisations is not improvised; it was built by the sociologist Kenneth D. Bailey, whose Social Entropy Theory (1990) is the most sustained attempt to ground macro-sociology in non-equilibrium thermodynamics.28 Bailey defined social entropy as "a measure of the natural decay of the structure or of the disappearance of distinctions within a social system" — the social analogue of the second law's tendency toward the levelling-out of differences.29 Crucially for the agreement axis, Bailey's theory includes an explicit "discussion of order and power," and treats the state of a social system as describable by "the degree of dissatisfaction or satisfaction with the social, political, and economic rules" — a social Gibbs free energy, the portion of the system's energy that is unavailable for work because it is bound up in unresolved conflict.30

Bailey's move lets us state the central identification precisely. A group's agreement is a low-entropy macrostate: many individuals occupying the same region of the space of possible goals, a highly ordered and improbable configuration carrying a great deal of structural information. A group's disagreement is a high-entropy macrostate: individuals dispersed across many goal-states, a disordered and probable configuration. In the language of information theory — where Shannon's H measures exactly the uncertainty over which of several states a system occupies — disagreement is high H: maximal when all goal-states are equally favoured, minimal when one commands consensus.31 The agreement axis, read this way, is an entropy axis inverted: high agreement = low social entropy; low agreement = high social entropy.

H = − Σ pi log pi
Shannon entropy over the distribution of a group's commitments across possible goal-states. When one goal commands consensus (one pi ≈ 1), H ≈ 0 — perfect agreement, minimum social entropy. When commitments are scattered evenly across many incompatible goals (all pi equal), H is maximal — deep disagreement, maximum social entropy. The vertical axis of the Stacey Matrix is, formally, −H.
In Plain Terms · Entropy, and its opposite

Entropy measures disorder — how many ways a system’s parts could be rearranged while looking the same from outside. High entropy is scattered, levelled, featureless; low entropy is ordered, structured, improbable. Negative entropy is order won against that tide. The whole argument turns on one translation: a group’s agreement is a low-entropy state, an improbable alignment of many wills; disagreement is entropy, the scattered state they slide back into the moment no work holds them together.

The Engine: Bifurcation, the Control Parameters, and the Politics of the Amplified Fluctuation

If agreement is negentropy and the zone of complexity is the dissipative regime, the obvious question is what moves a social system into that regime — and the answer names the levers Larned reached for under “modify structure” (Part II). A dissipative structure is tuned by a handful of control parameters, and for a human system the decisive three are diversity (the range of difference among the parts), connectivity (how densely they are coupled) and information flow (how freely signal moves between them). Set them too low and the system settles toward frozen near-equilibrium order — rigid, predictable, dead to novelty, the pathology of the rational corner. Set them too high and it shatters into uncorrelated noise, the social heat-death of the far corner. Between the two lies a narrow window — the edge this study has already named (Part IX) — where order through fluctuation becomes possible.27 The “zone of complexity,” then, is not a place an organisation simply is; it is a regime it is tuned into, by someone, through the setting of those parameters. And that someone is a question of power: management’s levers are the control parameters of a dissipative structure, and to set them — to widen or narrow diversity, to open or throttle connection and information — is to decide how far from equilibrium the whole organisation will be made to run.

Prigogine’s deepest result, though, concerns not the steady regime but the moment of change within it. Driven far enough from equilibrium, a system reaches a bifurcation point: a threshold at which its present order goes unstable and two or more new branches open before it. Which branch it takes is not fixed by the prevailing forces — those are balanced at the threshold — but by a fluctuation: a small, often microscopic chance event that, in the non-linear regime, is amplified until it “invade[s] the entire system” and carries it to one branch rather than another.27 At the bifurcation the system is exquisitely sensitive; a vanishing perturbation decides the macroscopic future. This is the precise thermodynamic content of the leader’s most ambitious move on Larned’s diagonal — “convene and intervene… to move to a new attractor” (Part II) — and of Zimmerman’s deliberate push to the edge. And it is the point at which the politics of perspective turns thermodynamically decisive. For near a bifurcation the question that governs the organisation’s whole future is: whose fluctuation gets amplified? Whose dissenting voice, whose deviant proposal, whose marginal and previously unheard perspective is the small event the system seizes and magnifies into its new order? At the edge, the fifteenth stone — the position no settled vantage could see — can become the entire garden. The manager who would steer is trying, precisely, to be that fluctuation: to inject the perturbation the system will amplify toward a chosen attractor. It is the gaze at once most powerful and most economical, for near the threshold a word, a meeting, a reframing can tip a field that tonnes of force could not move when it sat near equilibrium — the demon’s dream, order for almost nothing, available for one moment only, at the edge.

In Plain Terms · A bifurcation

Heat a shallow pan of liquid gently and nothing happens; heat it past a critical point and it suddenly organises itself into a lattice of rolling convection cells — and whether a given cell turns clockwise or anticlockwise is settled by a chance jiggle at the instant it forms. That instant is a bifurcation: a fork where a system, pushed hard enough, must jump to a new pattern, and where a tiny accident decides which pattern it jumps to. Organisations have such moments — a restructure, a crisis, a scandal, a charismatic arrival — when the old order gives way and some small thing, a single voice in a meeting, tips the whole into its next shape. Most of the time the field is deaf to you. At a bifurcation it is all ears.

Two consequences follow, and both are political. The first is that the branch taken is irreversible and historically contingent: the system carries forward the memory of the fluctuation that tipped it, and could, had a different accident been amplified, have settled into a quite different order — this is the arrow of time that Prigogine insisted dissipative systems possess.26 A consensus, therefore, is never a discovered truth; it is the frozen residue of whichever fluctuation happened to win at the last bifurcation — a path-dependent outcome, a spandrel (Part IX) that could have been otherwise. To present today’s agreement as natural, neutral, simply the way things are — the view from nowhere again — is to forget the contingent and political history of amplified and suppressed voices by which it actually formed. The second consequence is the one Bailey’s sociology makes explicit, and it returns us to the cost the four-box hides. Holding a system at low social entropy, far from equilibrium, demands continuous work: the negentropy of consensus must be paid for again and again, because the system is forever generating the disorder it must export. That work is the work of power — persuasion, incentive, coercion, and the patient labour of the gaze that watches, classifies and corrects — and the conflict it cannot resolve sits as what Bailey called a social free energy, bound and unavailable, a standing reservoir of the unagreed.30 Agreement is thus a cost borne by power and paid in exported entropy that falls on whoever stands outside it. The deletion of the agreement axis is the erasure of exactly this ledger: it re-presents a worked-for, power-maintained, historically contingent consensus as a free and natural fact — an order that looks as though it cost nothing and excluded no one, precisely because the axis on which the cost and the exclusion would show has been cut away.

The Synthesis: The Matrix as a Thermodynamic Field

With the two foundational pillars in place — and the bifurcation engine that animates them — the diagram can be re-read in full, and every zone acquires a thermodynamic meaning. The result is not a re-labelling but a unification: the two axes, which seemed merely juxtaposed, turn out to measure the two quantities that govern any dissipative structure — the gradient that drives it and the entropy it must manage.

Axis / zoneOrganisational meaningThermodynamic meaning
Certainty (horizontal)Knowability of cause and effectThe predictability gradient — how steeply the system's future can be inferred from its present; the "exergy" available for planned work
Agreement (vertical)Shared commitment to endsNegative social entropy (−H) — the orderliness of the group's distribution over goal-states; the negentropy bound into consensus
Rational / technical zonePlan, forecast, monitorNear-equilibrium order: low entropy, high predictability, sustained cheaply; linear response to perturbation
Political zoneCoalition, negotiation, powerHigh social entropy at low epistemic uncertainty: the disorder is in the distribution of ends, not in knowledge; order must be worked into being by power
Zone of complexityCreativity, innovation, emergenceThe far-from-equilibrium dissipative regime: order through fluctuation, where amplified variation produces novelty — the productive edge
"Anarchy" cornerBreakdown, to be avoidedThermodynamic equilibrium: maximum entropy, no gradient, no available free energy — not chaos but the social heat-death in which no work can be done

The Stacey Matrix re-read as a non-equilibrium thermodynamic field. The two axes measure the gradient that drives a dissipative structure and the entropy it must manage.

What the diagram draws as the storm is the calm of the grave; what it draws as the merely difficult middle is the only place anything lives.

The Cost of Order: Why Agreement Is Work

The thermodynamic reading yields its sharpest organisational consequence here, and it vindicates the Eliasian point of Part III with physical precision. The second law guarantees that an isolated system drifts toward maximum entropy — toward the levelling of distinctions. A diverse group of people, left to themselves, will naturally disperse into disagreement, because there are vastly more ways to disagree (high-entropy macrostates) than to agree (the rare low-entropy macrostate). Agreement is therefore not the default but the improbable achievement, and like every improbable low-entropy state it can be maintained only by continuous work — the work of persuasion, leadership, negotiation, coercion, ritual, shared narrative. This work is the social analogue of the energy a refrigerator must expend to keep its interior colder than the room: stop the work and the order decays.

And here the political axis and the thermodynamic axis become one. The "work" that maintains agreement against entropic drift is precisely what Part III called the management of power. Power, thermodynamically, is the capacity to do the work of holding a social system away from its high-entropy equilibrium — to keep a group's distribution over goals narrow when it would naturally broaden. This is why the agreement axis cannot be symmetric: a reading of "high agreement" tells you the system is in a low-entropy state but not what is paying for it — genuine shared conviction (a stable low-energy configuration) or the continuous expenditure of coercive power (a state held down by force, which will spring back the instant the work stops). The diagram's vertical axis, fully understood, is a measure of how much social entropy has been pushed out of the system, and its silent companion question is always: at what cost, and by whom?

A Necessary Objection · Arendt on Power That Cannot Be Stored

The free-energy reading meets its most serious objection in Hannah Arendt, whom Mowles reads closely. Power, for Arendt, is not a possession but an event: it "always arises as a potential when people get together to do something, but it disappears the moment they disperse; it cannot be stored in any way… and is changeable, unmeasurable and contingent."45 If power cannot be stored, the objection runs, how can agreement be a stock of negentropy held against decay? The answer does not weaken the thermodynamic reading; it states it more exactly. A dissipative structure stores no energy statically either. A flame, a convection cell, a living body, an aligned team — none holds a reserve; each persists only as a throughput, an order continuously remade out of energy passing through it, and each reverts to equilibrium the instant the flow is cut. Arendt's power that "disappears the moment they disperse" is precisely this: order as flow, not order as hoard.

So the apparent contradiction resolves into an identity. Agreement is not a tank of stored consensus to be drawn down but a fire to be fed; "the cost of order" is the cost of sustaining the throughput, not of guarding a stock; and the dissolution Arendt describes — dispersal — is simply the social name for the collapse to equilibrium that overtakes any dissipative structure deprived of its energy supply. Maxwell's dissipative teams (below) make the point experimentally: the complex state "decays back to equilibrium if not sustained." Arendt supplies, too, the non-linearity that the reading requires — "a comparatively small but well-organised group of men can rule almost indefinitely over large and populous countries"45 — the social form of order through fluctuation, in which a small, tightly-coupled perturbation is amplified until it organises a vast system. Far from refuting the physics, Arendt gives it its political face: power is the work, and the work is never done.

The Inversion Corrected

The thermodynamic reading overturns the diagram's single most common misunderstanding. The far corner — far from certainty, far from agreement — is universally labelled "chaos" or "anarchy" and drawn as the most dangerous, most turbulent place on the map, the region one must never enter. Thermodynamically this is exactly backwards. A state of maximum disagreement and maximum unpredictability is a state of maximum entropy — and maximum entropy is equilibrium: the most probable, most featureless, most dead configuration a system can occupy, in which every gradient has levelled, every distinction has dissolved, and no work can be done. It is not a storm. It is a still, cold pond. "Chaos," in the strict physical sense of turbulent, structure-generating dynamics, is not in that corner at all.

Where, then, is the turbulence? In the middle. The genuinely generative region — the true edge of chaos — is the zone of complexity, the far-from-equilibrium band in which the system is held away from the dead state and its fluctuations are amplified into new order. The diagram, read thermodynamically, has placed its danger and its life in precisely the wrong squares: what it draws as the terrifying corner is in fact the heat-death of the organisation, the most placid end-state there is; and what it draws as the merely difficult middle is the only place where anything alive happens. Maxwell's finding seals the correction from the other side — teams that flee the turbulent middle do not reach safety; they collapse into the false consensus of the dead corner, mistaking the stillness of equilibrium for the stillness of agreement.

The Third Pillar: Maxwell's Dissipative Teams

This reading is not a speculation laid over the management literature from outside; it has been tested empirically, at the small-group scale, by Chris Maxwell, whose University of Technology Sydney doctorate (2022) was developed in direct dialogue with Chris Mowles — who, Maxwell records, "introduced me to Ralph Stacey."32 Maxwell subjected five leadership teams to a sequence of quasi-experimental challenges and then to co-created sensemaking debriefs, and reconstructed the diagram as an Uncertainty Landscape: a phase diagram on which a team's state moves in response to the kind of problem it faces. His central empirical finding is the thermodynamic argument of this Part made flesh. Complex challenges, he found, drive a team out of its placid baseline into a complex state characterised by raised energy, tension and ambiguity — and that state is metastable and dissipative, "readily decaying back to the equilibrium state if not sustained." A team in the generative complex regime is not resting in a stable configuration; it is being held, at cost, away from an equilibrium into which it will collapse the moment the effort stops.32

The figure repays slow reading, because its axes are not Stacey’s relabelled but reconceived. The vertical no longer measures agreement but uncertainty about where we will end up — from the linear and known to the non-linear and unknowable; the horizontal measures uncertainty about how we will get there — from the deterministic to the random. The regions an organisation might treat as fixed categories — simple, complicated, complex — Maxwell treats as attractor states in a phase space: configurations a team falls into and is held within, with their own basins and their own stability. His teams showed two such attractors with a telling asymmetry. The complicated state (Attractor 1, the placid baseline) is deep and cheap to occupy; the complex state (Attractor 2, the generative regime) is shallow and expensive, and the looping arrows record the traffic between them — a provocation drives the team up into complexity, and gravity, in the form of discomfort and avoidance, rolls it back down. In the possibility-landscape vocabulary of the previous section, the complex attractor is the basin of richest adjacent possible, and the work of leadership is to keep the team from sliding out of it before the new order has formed.

The opening scene of Maxwell's thesis is, in miniature, the whole political reading of the agreement axis. Facilitating a software firm's leadership team, he watched a Commercial Director announce that the company's US expansion had been "a mistake initially and a failure since" — a remark Maxwell reads as "as much a power play as a contribution to strategic intent," loaded with the financial fact that the Australian business had been funding the US, and with the speaker's own rising "political stocks." In an instant "the room went quiet, activity stopped," attention swung "from task… to other," and "the energy and tension in the room had increased, the ambiguity and uncertainty had increased."32 A team sitting in the low-entropy rational corner — heads down, sorting tasks on a timeline — was driven by a single political intervention into the high-entropy political zone. No tool in "the entire strategy landscape," Maxwell observes, "was of any use in that moment." This is the certainty axis and the agreement axis moving together exactly as Part III predicted, observed live in a boardroom.

Primary Source · Maxwell, It's Just Not That Simple (UTS PhD, 2022)

Maxwell's most consequential finding inverts the management instinct to "resolve" disagreement, and it confirms the thermodynamic reading's sharpest claim — that there are two routes to agreement, one cheap and false, one costly and real. Teams that tried to escape the discomfort of the complex state — what he calls avoidance behaviours aimed at dissipating it — produced "sub-optimal decisions, false consensus or lack of progress to solution." Teams that could tolerate staying in the complex state, by contrast, "worked faster, achieved greater consensus and resolved the complex problems they were set."32 Read thermodynamically: collapsing prematurely to the low-entropy equilibrium of false consensus is the organisational heat-death dressed as harmony, while the genuine, durable agreement is the one bought by the work of remaining far from equilibrium. The agreement axis, Maxwell shows empirically, can be lowered by avoidance or earned by labour — and only the earned consensus holds.

Maxwell is also explicit about the physics, and his diagnosis underwrites Part IX of this study. The field, he argues in a section pointedly titled "We're envying the wrong physics," took its imagery from deterministic chaos when it should have looked to non-equilibrium thermodynamics; teams are not chaotic attractors but dissipative structures — "metastable, but not in equilibrium" — whose maintenance is a matter of "energy, work and entropy," and whose complex state decays unless fed.32 And his closing theme, "power in complex dynamics," returns the physics to the politics: the energy that holds a team in its generative state is supplied, and withheld, through the exercise of power — the same identification this study makes when it calls power the capacity to do the work of holding a social system from equilibrium. Maxwell took the diagram back to the far-from-equilibrium language of Stacey's own 1992 pre-history and gave it, for the first time, a tested empirical form at the scale on which teams actually work.

The Demon and the Gaze — Why Looking Is Never Free

It is a coincidence too good to waste that the management scholar whose dissipative teams anchored the last section shares a name with the most famous observer in the history of physics. In 1867 James Clerk Maxwell imagined a tiny being stationed at a trapdoor between two gas-filled chambers, able to see each approaching molecule and open the door only for the fast ones going one way and the slow ones the other — sorting hot from cold and so, apparently, lowering the entropy of a closed system for nothing, in flat violation of the second law.69 For a century this demon haunted thermodynamics, because its only apparatus was a gaze: it did no work on the molecules; it merely looked, and sorted. And looking, surely, is free.

It is not. The exorcism of the demon, carried out by Leó Szilard, Rolf Landauer and Charles Bennett between 1929 and 1982, is among the deepest results of the last century, and it turns precisely on the cost of the gaze.69 To sort, the demon must record what it sees — fast or slow, this molecule, then the next — and that record accumulates in a finite memory. To go on operating it must eventually erase the old record; and Landauer proved that erasing a single bit of information must dissipate at least kT ln 2 of heat into the environment.69 The entropy the demon appears to remove by sorting is repaid, exactly, by the entropy of erasing what it had to know in order to sort. The second law is saved — “but at what a cost!” — and the lesson is lapidary: information is physical, and there is no free lunch in looking. As one statement of the principle has it, no physical process has as its sole result the erasure of information.69

In Plain Terms · The demon and the cost of knowing

Maxwell’s demon is an imaginary sorter that watches molecules and lets only the fast ones through a door, seeming to make order — hot on one side, cold on the other — out of nothing. The catch, found a century later, is that to sort it must remember what it saw, and to keep sorting it must eventually forget — and forgetting, physically, costs energy and sheds heat (Landauer’s kT ln 2 per erased bit). So the order bought by an act of observation is never free: it is paid for in the physics of information. Looking has a price.

Read the Stacey Matrix in this light and a startling identity appears. The manager who lifts a situation onto the field — this issue is near-certain and well-agreed, route it to the rational corner; that one is complex, treat it otherwise — is performing exactly the demon’s operation: an act of informed observation that sorts a turbulent field into ordered bins and appears to produce decidability for nothing. The matrix is the trapdoor; classification is the gaze; and the promise of the popular four-box — look, sort, decide — is the promise of order for free. But the thermodynamics of information says it cannot be free, and the politics of perspective says it is never neutral — and these prove to be the same objection seen from two sides.

For the act of looking-and-sorting is, in the other vocabulary, a gaze, and a century of theory has taught that the gaze is an instrument of power, never a neutral registration. Michel Foucault named the disciplinary examination the operation that “makes each individual a ‘case’: a case which at one and the same time constitutes an object for a branch of knowledge and a hold for a branch of power.”70 That is exactly what the matrix does to a situation: it constitutes it as a case — an object the manager may now claim to know, and so a thing the manager may now hold. Foucault’s emblem for this was Bentham’s panopticon, the tower whose single eye sees into every cell while itself remaining unseen — the gaze that observes without being observed, in which “visibility is a trap.”70 The four-box is a panopticon in two dimensions: it surveys the whole organisational field from one elevated, unmarked vantage and files each conflict away as a type, while the one doing the filing — their position, their interest, their power — stays, like the panoptic watcher, carefully outside the frame. It is, once more, Nagel’s view from nowhere, and Hockney’s one-point perspective in which “you yourself are not even in the picture at all” (Overture) — here unmasked as an apparatus of governance.

Sartre and Berger supply the half Foucault’s prisoners cannot: the gaze is reciprocal, and it positions the looker too. Sartre’s Look turns the one it falls upon into an object, “frozen, objectified, alienated” — but the same Look, when the looker is caught at the keyhole, reveals that they were always visible too, always somewhere.71 Berger put it with great economy: “we never look at just one thing; we are always looking at the relation between things and ourselves.”71 There is no gaze from nowhere; the demon stands inside the box it sorts; the manager is a participant in the field they claim only to map (Mowles, Rodgers; Parts IV–V). To pretend otherwise — to wield the matrix as though from the panoptic tower — is not an epistemic stance but a political one: it disappears the chooser and naturalises the chooser’s authority.

And here the two threads, the thermodynamic and the political, close into a single knot — the knot this whole study has been tying. What is the deletion of the agreement axis, the field’s most extreme morphology, in the demon’s terms? It is an erasure. The agreement axis is the dimension along which an organisation’s disagreement, its dissent, its distribution of power are recorded; it is the political memory of the field. To collapse the matrix into the four-box is to throw that record away — to perform, on the body politic, the very operation Landauer placed at the heart of the demon’s cycle. And Landauer’s law is merciless about what erasure can achieve: it cannot make the disorder disappear. By the second law the entropy erased from the record is not abolished but dissipated into the environment. So too here. The disagreement the four-box deletes from the diagram does not vanish from the organisation; it is exported — into the shadow system, the corridor, the resignation, the quiet refusal (Parts IV, VI) — precisely the entropy the diagram claimed to have resolved, now doing its work unseen, like the heat a computer sheds when it forgets.
