The deletion is a demon that erases the memory of its own sorting so that the sort will look free; a panopticon that hides not merely the watcher but the watching. And the cost, paid in displaced political heat, falls where it always falls — on whoever occupies the position the gaze declined to record: the dissenter unmarked on the chart, the fifteenth stone, the kawaramono kept off the veranda (Overture).

This is why the politics of perspective and the thermodynamics of disagreement are not two readings of the matrix but one. To observe is to sort; to sort is to spend (Landauer) and to position (Foucault); and to hide that one has spent and positioned — by deleting the axis along which the spending and the positioning would show — is the most economical political act available, for it disguises a costly, situated gaze as a free and neutral fact. The matrix, read whole, is the standing refutation of that disguise. It says: every act of looking at an organisation is an act within it; every sort has a price; and the dimension you erase to make the price invisible is exactly the dimension where power lives.

The Gaze That Makes Its Object — When the Map Breeds the Territory

The demon’s lesson was that the gaze costs. There is a second lesson, stranger and more dangerous, and it is the deepest reach of the politics of perspective: in the hands of an institution, the gaze does not merely register its object — it creates it. A perspective that sees partially is one thing; a perspective with the power to make real what it sees is another, and the Stacey Matrix, as an instrument of management, has exactly that power.

Jean Baudrillard gave the principle its most violent formulation. In the order he called the precession of simulacra, “the map precedes the territory”: the model comes first, and the real is generated afterward, in its image — a real “without origin or reality.”53 A simulation becomes dangerous, he warned, not when it imitates reality but when it replaces it. This is the latent destiny of any diagram powerful enough to be believed. Deploy the matrix long enough in an organisation — route the budgets, the meetings, the methods by it — and it stops describing the organisational field and begins to manufacture it. The chart ceases to be a map of the territory and becomes the template from which the territory is cut.

Ian Hacking supplied the mechanism, and named it precisely. Classifying people, he showed, sets off a looping effect: “naming has real effects on people, and changes in people have real effects on subsequent classifications,” so that the category and the categorised “emerge simultaneously and in interaction.”72 To call a syndrome into being is, in a real sense, to make up a kind of person who then learns to inhabit the name — what Hacking called dynamic nominalism, the doctrine that “creating new ways of classifying people changes the subjects’ relations with themselves.”72 The matrix makes up kinds of situations, and the loop runs just as surely. Tell a team it sits in “the zone of complexity” and it begins to perform complexity; file an issue as “simple” and it receives the simple handling that keeps it simple; stamp a programme “best practice” and it is managed into the shape the stamp foretold. The classified loop back into the class. And Hacking located the first dynamic nominalist exactly where this study began — in Nietzsche, who wrote in The Gay Science that “unspeakably more depends on what things are called than on what they are… creating new names… is enough to create new ‘things.’”72,65 The gaze that names is a gaze that makes.

This is why Foucault’s panopticon does its work without chains. Under a gaze that classifies, the watched begin to watch themselves; the category becomes a norm against which each measures and corrects their own conduct, so that the discipline is at last performed by the disciplined.70 The cybernetic and organisational traditions reached the same place from the inside: Heinz von Foerster, that “the environment as we perceive it is our invention” (Overture);67 Karl Weick, that managers “enact” the very environments they then take themselves to be reacting to — constructing, singling out and demolishing the features of their surroundings, and living thereafter inside the enacted world (Part I).46 Even physics concedes the point at its own foundation: “what we observe is not nature itself,” said Heisenberg, “but nature exposed to our method of questioning.” There is no innocent registration anywhere; to question is to shape the answer. The matrix, then, is not a mirror held up to the organisation. It is a mould.

Put the mould together with the demon and the whole mechanism stands revealed. The deletion of the agreement axis — the field’s most extreme morphology — is not only an erasure of the political record (the Landauer cost of the last section); it is a shaping of what the territory is permitted to become. A map with no axis for disagreement, deployed as a mould, breeds an organisation that cannot see its own disagreement: dissent arrives as a category error, conflict reads as a fault to be corrected, and the field loops, obediently, into the docile and depoliticised form the four-box depicts. The map that deletes politics manufactures a territory that has forgotten it is political — which is the most complete victory a politics of perspective can win, because it is a victory that erases the evidence that any choosing took place.

The thermodynamic translation is exact, and it closes the circle the gaze has drawn. A self-fulfilling classification is an imposed constraint: it does not discover a basin of attraction in the field, it digs one, forcing the organisation toward a single default form and collapsing the space of shapes it might otherwise have taken (the morphospace of Part IX). This is order bought not by the patient work that maintains a dissipative structure far from equilibrium, but by amputation — by cutting away possibility until only the depicted form remains. And amputated disorder, as the demon taught, is not abolished but displaced: the variety the mould forbids on the chart reappears as the exported political heat of the previous section — the shadow system, the corridor, the resignation. The gaze that creates its object and the gaze that cannot escape its cost are one gaze, and the deleted axis is the single place where both the creation and the cost are hidden from view.

If there is a defence, it is the one the garden and the joiner have been showing us all along. Against the mould stands Haraway’s situated knowledge, which insists the map confess where it was drawn and by whom (Part I);59 against the single template, Hockney’s hundred eyes and Nietzsche’s objectivity-of-the-many (Overture); against the territory that cannot see its own map, Korzybski’s minimal, saving distinction — the map is not the territory (Part I).50 To keep the agreement axis is precisely to keep the territory able to see, and to contest, the map laid over it. A field that can still locate its own disagreements is a field that has not yet been wholly made up — one in which the fifteenth stone, though unseen from any single seat, has not been quarried away.

The Field as a Possibility Landscape

These two movements — the gaze that costs and the gaze that creates — have carried the field deep into its politics; one feature of its formal geography remains to be drawn. The energy landscape developed earlier in this Part has a twin, from the same complexity science, that completes the picture: a possibility landscape. The notion descends from Sewall Wright, who in 1932 proposed to picture evolution as movement across a fitness landscape — a space in which every possible configuration is a point, its "height" is its viability, and adaptation is a walk uphill toward peaks of fitness.47 The image has since become one of the master metaphors of complexity: a topography of the possible, on which a system's present location fixes not only how well it is doing but, decisively, what it can become next. To read the Stacey field as such a landscape is to ask a question the management literature never puts to it — not merely "how much work holds this group together?" but "what futures are reachable from where the group now stands?" Each region of the diagram then ceases to be a style of management and becomes a distinct possibility structure: a different answer to what can be done, and what can be reached, from here.

Ruggedness, Hill-Climbing, and the Adjacent Possible

Stuart Kauffman gave Wright's picture quantitative teeth with the NK model, in which a single parameter — K, the degree of interdependence among a system's parts — tunes the ruggedness of the landscape. At K = 0 the parts are independent and the surface is smooth, with one peak that simple hill-climbing always finds; as K rises toward its maximum the surface buckles into a maze of summits and valleys, "maximally rugged" and uncorrelated, on which a searcher who merely optimises locally is stranded on a minor peak.38 This is the formal difference between the diagram's regions. The rational corner — low interdependence, agreed ends, known means — is a smooth, single-peaked landscape: the optimum is real and reachable, and "best practice" is just hill-climbing toward it. The zone of complexity is a rugged landscape: ends and means are densely interdependent (K is high), there is no single summit to ascend, and a group that optimises its present position will lock onto a false peak. On rugged terrain exploration beats optimisation — which is the precise, formal reason the generative zone rewards disagreement, variation and search over early consensus. To agree too soon on rugged ground is to climb the nearest hillock and call it the mountain.

Kauffman's deeper contribution is the adjacent possible: at any instant a system has access not to all conceivable futures but only to the set of states reachable in a single step from where it stands — and as it moves into one of those, the horizon of the reachable recedes and opens further.38 The adjacent possible is the local neighbourhood of the possibility landscape, and its size is not constant across the field. The equilibrium corner has an adjacent possible of essentially zero: from a state in which every gradient has levelled and every distinction has dissolved, nothing new is within reach. The zone of complexity has the richest adjacent possible of the whole diagram — poised far from equilibrium, the system sits at a point from which a small move opens many neighbouring states, and each move opens more. Steven Johnson popularised this as the engine of innovation; here it is the exact sense in which the generative zone is generative. It is not that novelty is more likely there in some vague way; it is that the region far from equilibrium is the region of maximal access to the adjacent possible, and the dead corner is the region of none.

In Plain Terms · The adjacent possible

At any moment only certain next steps are actually reachable from where you stand — not every conceivable future, only the ones a single move away. That set of reachable nexts is the adjacent possible, and it widens as you explore: each door you open reveals others that were shut before. The point that matters here is stark — in the dead corner of the field almost no doors are open; in the zone of complexity the room is full of them.

Canalization, Constraint, and the Shaping of the Possible

A possibility landscape is not handed down fixed. Conrad Waddington's epigenetic landscape (1957) pictured development as a marble rolling down a surface of branching valleys — chreods, from the Greek for "necessary path" — whose depth and steepness canalise the trajectory, buffering it against perturbation and channelling it toward a limited set of outcomes.48 Waddington's decisive stroke lay underneath the surface: its shape, he drew, was pulled into being by a web of guy-ropes staked to genes below, so that the topography itself could be reshaped even as the marble ran. Translated to organisations, this is the cartography of habit and of power. Routine, culture and "best practice" are the deep, canalised valleys of the corporate landscape — the chreods down which behaviour runs without deliberation — and the guy-ropes that hold those valleys in place are exactly Rodgers's embedded power and Mowles's thought collectives from Part IV. To control the topography of the possibility landscape, deepening some valleys and flattening others, is a large part of what organisational power is: it sets the chreods along which everyone else's sense-making runs, before any explicit decision is taken.

Constraint, on this picture, is double-edged. Alicia Juarrero distinguishes constraints that merely restrict from enabling constraints that, precisely by ruling some things out, make new things possible — as the banks of a river create a current no open plain could sustain.49 Agreement is an enabling constraint of just this kind: a group that narrows its possibility space by settling some questions frees the energy to reach further on others, exactly as a riverbed concentrates a flow. This reframes "the cost of order" one final time. To hold a group's agreement is to sculpt the terrain — to keep the valleys worth descending deep and the false peaks shallow — and that sculpting is the continuous work of power. And it exposes, in landscape terms, what the agile mutation did when it deleted the agreement axis: it did not simplify the map, it flattened the possibility landscape, collapsing a two-dimensional terrain of reachable futures onto a single technical line, and erasing from view the very dimension along which an organisation's possibilities are opened, closed, and fought over.

The Energy Landscape Is a Possibility Landscape

The two readings of the field are one surface seen twice. Free energy — the capacity to do work, the distance from equilibrium — is, in the possibility register, the capacity to reach new states: the measure of how much adjacent possible a system can afford. A system at equilibrium has neither: no free energy, so it can do no work; no adjacent possible, so it can become nothing new. That is the flat, featureless corner the diagram mislabels "anarchy." A system held far from equilibrium has both at once — the energy to act and the open horizon of the reachable — which is why the zone of complexity is simultaneously the high-energy region of the thermodynamic reading and the high-possibility region of this one. The agreement axis read as negative entropy and the same axis read as position on a possibility landscape are the identical quantity in two vocabularies; and in both, what holds a living organisation away from the dead flat corner, keeping its futures open, is the unceasing work of power.

Two hundred and forty copies did not enrich the map. They crowded into one dead corner of it — and called the crowding success.

The Morphology of the Field, and Its Dynamics

If the field is a landscape, it is also a morphospace — and seeing it as one explains both the diagram’s mutations and its motion. The term is David Raup’s: in 1966, extending D’Arcy Thompson’s insight that the forms of nature are deformations of a few simple models, Raup mapped all geometrically possible mollusc shells into a space whose axes were the parameters of coiling, so that every conceivable shell was a point and every actual shell a point that happened to be occupied.55 The signal finding of theoretical morphology is that most of the morphospace is empty: of all the forms the parameters permit, only a fraction are ever realised, and the vacant regions mark constraints — developmental, constructional, ecological — on what can actually be built and survive.55 The Stacey field is a morphospace of organisational forms: each point a possible mode of acting-together, fixed by two parameters (certainty, agreement), its regions differently occupiable. The far “anarchy” corner is empty not because it is forbidden to draw but because it cannot be stably inhabited — the morphospace equivalent of a shell that cannot bear its own weight. The diagram’s error was to treat an unoccupiable region as merely one more box.

In Plain Terms · A morphospace

Picture a map of every shape a thing could possibly take — every conceivable shell, every conceivable organisation — each point a different possible form. That map is a morphospace. Its great lesson is that most of it is empty: of all the forms the rules permit, only a few are ever built, because the rest cannot hold together. Read the Stacey field this way and its “anarchy” corner stops being a place to fear and becomes a place that simply cannot be inhabited.

Theoretical morphology supplies a second distinction the diagram’s history demands — between diversity (how many forms exist) and disparity (how much of the morphospace they span) — and in the diagram’s case the two have moved in opposite directions. The proliferation of the matrix into some two hundred and forty imitators is a vast gain in diversity; but it is a gain confined almost entirely to one small, impoverished region of the form-space, the four-box corner, so that in disparity — the true range of distinct forms realised — the field has contracted. Morphologists find, too, that occupancy is often greatest early in a lineage and declines thereafter, disparity peaking before diversity does. The diagram has run exactly that course, in mirror image of the progress it imagines for itself: its disparity was highest at the origin, when the five-zone original, Larned’s continuous diagonal and Zimmerman’s graded continuum all coexisted, and it has fallen ever since toward the single squared, axis-less form that now saturates the field. What presents itself as the triumph of a tool is, morphologically, the collapse of a form-space.

D’Arcy Thompson’s deeper device, the transformation grid — by which the form of one species is derived from another’s by warping a coordinate mesh56 — is also the key to the diagram’s own history. The mutations traced in Part VIII are transformation grids applied to a diagram: the five-zone original deformed into the four-box grid (a coarsening that tears the central zone of complexity in two and erases the diagonal), that grid deformed again into Larned’s continuous diagonal, and again into Maxwell’s attractor landscape. To study the matrix’s radical and extreme morphology is to study these deformations — and to notice that one of them, the four-box coarsening, warped the form so violently that it severed the axis giving the space its second dimension, flattening a morphospace into a list.

And the field is not static; it is a phase space. In dynamical-systems terms a phase space is the space of all possible states of a system, across which it traces trajectories pulled toward attractors — regions it tends to fall into and stay.57 Maxwell’s finding (above) is exactly this: the complicated and complex regions are attractor basins, and a team is a point moving between them. Larned’s diagonal is a trajectory across the space; the “deliberate edge” move is the injection of energy that carries the point over a boundary. And those boundaries matter precisely. The dashed lines dividing the zones are not decorative: to cross one is a bifurcation — a qualitative, often discontinuous change in behaviour as a parameter (certainty, or agreement) passes a critical value.57 Because the zones meet at sharp borders rather than blending, the relevant bifurcations are of the border-collision kind, in which the dynamics change abruptly the instant the border is crossed — which is why an organisation does not drift smoothly from the rational corner into the political zone but is tipped into it, in a moment, by a single intervention (Maxwell’s Commercial Director above; Zimmerman’s “deliberate edge”). The return is not the mirror of the descent: like many driven systems the field exhibits hysteresis — a team tipped into the political zone is not restored to the rational corner merely by restoring certainty, because the path back runs through a different sequence of states than the path in, which is why de-escalating a politicised conflict costs so much more than the provocation that began it. The radical reading of the matrix is therefore not a sorting grid at all but a morphodynamic object: a morphospace of organisational forms, deformed through history by transformation, and traversed in real time by trajectories that fall into attractors and jump at bifurcations.

In Plain Terms · Attractors and bifurcations

Picture a landscape of hills and valleys with a ball rolling on it. The valleys are attractors — the resting places the ball keeps rolling back into. A bifurcation is the moment when, as you tilt the landscape only slightly, a valley suddenly splits, disappears, or appears, and the ball’s fate flips all at once rather than by degrees. That abrupt switch is exactly what happens when a single remark tips a calm team into open conflict.

Why the Field Is Mostly Empty — Constraint, Convergence, and the Default Form

Raup worked with two kinds of morphospace, and the distinction sharpens the whole critique. An empirical morphospace is built upward from measured forms; a theoretical morphospace is generated downward from a few abstract parameters, so that it contains every form the parameters permit, whether or not anything has ever realised it.61 The Stacey field is a theoretical morphospace of the leanest possible kind — two parameters, certainty and agreement — which is exactly why it can display regions, like the far corner, that no organisation occupies, and exactly why it is so seductive: a two-parameter space looks elegant, total and complete. But the elegance is bought by omission. Raup generated the entire kingdom of shells from a handful of coiling parameters, and the price was every dimension those parameters left out; the diagram's two axes likewise generate a clean, total-seeming space at the cost of the dimensions The Living Map named — relational history, emotional weather, the standing distribution of power (Part I). A theoretical morphospace is only ever as honest as it is explicit about what it has chosen not to vary.

Theoretical morphology also explains why such a space is mostly empty, and its three classic answers map cleanly onto the field.61 Constructional constraint — some forms cannot be built — is the far corner: a body of people at maximum disagreement and maximum unpredictability cannot be assembled into a working organisation at all; it is the social equilibrium of Part VII, a form with no integrity to hold. Functional constraint — some buildable forms can do no work — is the anarchy zone read dynamically: even were it reached for a moment, it performs nothing, dissipating before it can act. And phylogenetic, or historical, constraint — the forms actually realised are limited by what came before — is the diagram's own descent: the four-box is not generated afresh from Stacey's parameters but inherited, by a line of copying, from Zimmerman's overlay (Part II), which is why it reproduces her categories and not Stacey's zones. What an organisation can imagine itself to be is constrained less by the space of the possible than by the diagram it happens to have inherited.

The most striking morphological fact about the diagram's history, though, is convergence. In evolution, unrelated lineages arrive independently at the same form whenever one shape is overwhelmingly favoured — the camera eye evolved many times, the streamlined body again and again. The two hundred and forty variants are convergence of precisely this kind: project management, agile software, health-care reform and the consultancies each arrived, independently, at the same squared four-box, because one form is overwhelmingly favoured for transmission. The two-by-two is the default cognitive template of managerial thought — the shape a diagram collapses into when no force holds it in a richer configuration — so diagram-space has a developmental attractor, and the attractor is the four-box. This is the morphological restatement of the whole study: the field's disparity collapsed not because the four-box is true but because it is the form managerial diagram-space rolls downhill into, the deep chreod of the genre.

In Plain Terms · A spandrel

In architecture, a spandrel is the roughly triangular space left over where two rounded arches meet beneath a dome — not designed for itself, but the unavoidable by-product of building arches at all. Renaissance painters then filled those leftover spaces so gloriously that one might mistake the dome for having been raised in order to display them. Stephen Jay Gould borrowed the word for evolution: a trait that is a structural by-product rather than something selection built — and which may later be pressed into a use it was never made for (an exaptation). The word is a warning against a seductive error: seeing a use, and assuming the thing must have been designed for it.

One last borrowing from the morphologists names the diagram's strangest feature exactly. Gould and Lewontin warned against reading every trait as an adaptation; a great many are spandrels — byproducts of structure, later pressed into a use they were never built for, an exaptation.62 The simple/complicated/complex vocabulary is just such a spandrel. Zimmerman minted it as a pedagogical byproduct — a way of walking lay audiences through Stacey's zones — and it was later exapted, cut from its axes, into the sorting label of the agile four-box, a load it was never built to bear. What travelled the field was not the structure but its spandrel; and a spandrel, asked to carry the thrust of the arch, gives way. That is the diagram's entire career compressed into a single morphological word.

What the Thermodynamic Reading Resolves

Three persistent puzzles dissolve under this reading. (1) Why the two axes belong together: they are not an arbitrary pairing but the two governing quantities of any dissipative structure — the driving gradient (certainty) and the managed entropy (agreement). (2) Why the political zone is irreducible: disagreement is entropy, and no amount of knowledge (movement along the certainty axis) can lower the entropy of a value-conflict (position on the agreement axis); only work — power — can. (3) Why complexity is generative rather than merely difficult: the zone of complexity is the far-from-equilibrium regime where, by Prigogine's theorem, order arises through amplified fluctuation. Zimmerman's instinct to "increase disagreement to nudge the system to the edge of chaos" is, in thermodynamic terms, exactly right: to create, you must drive the system away from its placid equilibrium and feed the fluctuations.

Synthesis · The Political Is the Thermodynamic Seen From Inside

The bridge can be put under a single figure: the matrix as an energetic substrate — the agreement axis read as a field of social valences, the whole diagram as a quasi-stationary equilibrium that is always already moving. The political is the thermodynamic seen from inside: what a physicist describes as the work required to hold a system away from equilibrium, a participant experiences as the friction of persuasion, the cost of coalition, the exhaustion of holding a fractious group to a common course. Disagreement is entropy felt from within.

◆
VIII The History · Part VIII

The Genealogy, 1986–2026

"This is not knowledge creation; that is knowledge destruction. What remains is homeopathic knowledge." — Frank Habermann

The thermodynamic reading explains not only the diagram but its history. A low-entropy artefact — five precise zones, two carefully distinguished axes, a held-open paradox — was released into a vast field of human interaction, and like any ordered structure left without maintenance it decayed toward disorder, each adaptation raising the entropy of its meaning until almost nothing remained but a name and a shape. Forty years trace this dissipation through six epochs.

The Pre-History (1986–1992)

The diagram did not yet exist when its foundations were laid. The Chaos Frontier (1991) and Managing the Unknowable (1992) argued — in books almost devoid of figures — for "bounded instability," the far-from-equilibrium condition in which creativity becomes possible.33 The whole later programme is named in the chapter titles of the 1992 book: "Stable Instability: Creating the Far-from-Equilibrium Organization." The idea the diagram would later spatialise, and the thermodynamic language Part IV recovered, were present before any matrix — which is why Stacey could later burn the figure without burning the thought.

The Origin and the Faithful Transmissions (1993–2002)

The diagram appears in 1993, matures into five zones in 1996, and is transmitted — faithfully — through the documents read closely in Part II: Zimmerman's Edgeware work (1998), her NCCMT aide and the Larned diagonal (2001), the Glouberman–Zimmerman cake / rocket / child tripartition for the Romanow Commission (2002), and the Praxis project-management entry. This epoch preserves the political axis and the five zones; the entropy of the diagram's meaning is still low.

The Mutation (2004)

Schwaber — "The Science of Scrum"2004

founding [extreme] morphology

Axes renamed: Requirements Complexity (vertical) × Technology Complexity (horizontal)

In Agile Project Management with Scrum, in a chapter titled "The Science of Scrum," Ken Schwaber reproduced the diagram (in Zimmerman's form) and renamed the axes.8 Habermann's objection is unanswerable: "Why should uncertainty only relate to technology (and not also to requirements)? And why should people only be able to disagree about requirements (and not also about the technology)?"34 The renaming deletes the political axis (Parts III–IV) and, with it, the thermodynamic content of Part VII: a diagram that once measured social entropy now measures two kinds of technical difficulty. This is the entropy of the diagram's meaning rising sharply — the moment the structure begins to dissolve.

The Migrations (2005–2020)

The diagram then migrates into field after field, each leaving a variant of distinct fidelity, catalogued here:

VariantDateAxis renamingFidelity
Innes / BJGP (clinical consultation)2005certainty × agreement (retained)Disciplined transfer
Halpin & Hanlon (Legitimate/Shadow)2008retained; edge of chaos as optimumTheoretical deepening
Appelo / Management 3.02008structure (understandable↔hard) × behaviour (predictable↔chaotic)Rival re-axing
"Clarity" / von Bergen (German consulting)2015+clarity of goal ("what") × clarity of path ("how")Dilution
VUCA / post-COVID crisis versions2020+four-box agile form + topical overlayTopical overlay
Kraaijenbrink (coloured four-box + edge curve)c. 2018four zones, edge of chaos as danger-boundaryCosmetic consolidation
Maxwell (Uncertainty Landscape)2022determinism↔randomness × linear↔chaos; teams as dissipativeScholarly reconstruction

The "clarity" family is the diagram at its most diluted: Habermann singles out the substitution of "clarity of the goal and the way to get there" as "the crudest modification," unobjectionable in itself but inheriting a borrowed scientific authority once attached to Stacey's name.34 The Maxwell reconstruction is the opposite pole — the diagram taken back to the thermodynamics of its pre-history and rebuilt with genuine apparatus, demonstrating that serious reconstruction remains possible even after the popular corruption.

The Two Plates as a Single Argument

Set side by side, Plate I and Plate II are the visual record of this study's central claim. In the source diagram (Plate I), the second zone reads, in Stacey's own hand, "Political decision making & control; compromise, negotiation; dominant coalitions" — the entire apparatus of Parts III and IV named on the face of the artefact. The diagonal carries the garbage-can vocabulary of "unprogrammable decision making, outcomes rather than solutions" and "agenda building"; the third zone names "ideological control." Politics, power, and the negotiation of ends are not read into the diagram by this study; they are written on it by its author.

In the consolidated descendant (Plate II), every one of those terms is gone or demoted. Five graded zones have collapsed to four boxes; "dominant coalitions" and "ideological control" have vanished; "Political Decision-making" survives as a pale sub-caption inside "Complicated," no longer a zone of its own. The transformation from Plate I to Plate II is the deletion of the agreement axis's political content — performed not by argument but by redesign, one tidy quadrant at a time. The genealogy of Part VIII is, in the end, the story of how the organisation got from the first picture to the second, and what it lost on the way: precisely the half of the diagram that asked something uncomfortable of power.

The Disappearance and the Afterlife (2000–2026)

While the variants multiplied, the diagram vanished from its author's own books. From 2000, with Griffin and Shaw, Stacey developed complex responsive processes — on which complexity is the permanent condition rather than a zone, and there is no observer outside the process — and progressively dropped the figure across editions of the textbook, which reached its seventh edition (with Mowles) in 2016.35 By 2009 he was stating plainly: "life is complex all the time, not just on those occasions which can be characterised as being far from certainty and far from agreement."36 Yet the abandoned diagram was, by one widely-cited count, adapted more than 240 times and continues to spread. Habermann names the mechanism the beam effect: "the more undefined a framework's meaning, the broader its apparent scope of application."34 In the thermodynamic idiom of this study: the diagram proliferated because each adaptation raised the entropy of its meaning, and a maximally high-entropy meaning — one that can signify almost anything — diffuses everywhere, exactly as a gas fills any container. Plasticity and fidelity are inversely related; the 240 variants are 240 stages in the dissipation of one idea.

The Morphological Determination

The diagram did not evolve in the Darwinian sense of accumulating adaptive improvement; it dissipated in the thermodynamic sense of decaying toward disorder. Each variant traded a unit of conceptual content for a gain in transmissibility, until what remained was a name and a shape that could mean almost anything. The proliferation is, with some irony, the best available empirical demonstration of the very dynamics the diagram was first drawn to describe: an artefact released into a field of human interaction, amplified and deformed by countless local re-interpretations, with no author controlling the global pattern — an emergent result bearing its founder's name without being, in any conceptual sense, his descendant.

◆
IX The Science · Part IX

The Edge of Chaos

"Our experiment produced very different results, and we suggest that the interpretation of the original results is not correct." — Mitchell, Crutchfield & Hraber, 1993

The diagram borrows its most charged feature — the "edge of chaos" boundary — from complexity science, and a study that has just rested its central argument on thermodynamics is obliged to ask whether the borrowed science is sound. The answer is double: the specific scientific claim was refuted within complexity science, and the diagram places it backwards. But the thermodynamic reading of Part IV survives both problems, because it does not depend on the contested claim.

What the Edge of Chaos Was

The phrase comes from Christopher Langton's 1990 work on cellular automata.37 Langton defined a parameter, λ, as the fraction of an automaton's transition rules mapping to a non-quiescent state; sweeping λ from 0 to 1 carries behaviour from frozen order through a narrow critical region into chaos. His hypothesis, building on Norman Packard, was that automata capable of complex computation are found near the critical λ, because complex computation requires storing and transmitting information over long ranges — which demands the long transients found near phase transitions. Stuart Kauffman's parallel work on Boolean networks gave the idea biological reach with the phrase "order for free."38

The Refutation and the Misplacement

The Refutation Within the Science

In 1993 Melanie Mitchell, James Crutchfield and Peter Hraber, at the Santa Fe Institute itself, repeated Packard's genetic-algorithm experiment and found that evolved rules cluster not at Langton's critical λ but near λ = ½ — and for reasons unrelated to "computation at criticality": the clustering is an artefact of the task's symmetry and of drift in the genetic algorithm. Their conclusion is blunt: "the interpretation of the original results is not correct."39 The strong, mechanistic edge-of-chaos hypothesis did not survive the decade of its birth. The qualitative intuition — that complexity peaks between order and disorder — survives as a heuristic; the precise claim does not.

The diagram compounds this by placing the edge of chaos backwards. In Langton's usage the edge is the zone of maximum capacity — the place to be. In every populated version of the matrix it is drawn as the dashed boundary one must not cross into "anarchy," so the creative work happens safely below it. Part VII already corrected this from the thermodynamic side: the genuinely generative region is the far-from-equilibrium zone of complexity, while the far corner is not chaos but equilibrium. The one strand of the management literature that gets it right is Halpin and Hanlon's, in which an organisation at optimum interaction "sits at the Edge of Chaos in a state of bounded instability" — the edge as optimum, exactly as Langton and Prigogine alike intended.40

Why the Thermodynamic Reading Survives

Crucially, the argument of Part VII does not rest on the refuted λ-hypothesis. Prigogine's dissipative-structure theory — order through fluctuation far from equilibrium — is independently established thermodynamics, not the contested cellular-automata result. The diagram's borrowed phrase ("edge of chaos") rests on shaky science; the diagram's deep structure (a gradient axis and an entropy axis, a generative far-from-equilibrium band, an equilibrium dead-corner) rests on the secure thermodynamics of Prigogine and the social-entropy theory of Bailey. The science the diagram advertised was overturned; the science it unknowingly embodied was not.

◆
X The Mirror · Part X

The Two Matrices, and the Mirror

"The matrix is the hypothetical web of communication and relationship in a given group… the common shared ground upon which all communications rest." — S. H. Foulkes, 1964

There is a second matrix in this history, older and almost exactly opposite — and it completes the argument, because it is the matrix Stacey embraced after he burned his own, and it descends from the same Eliasian root that underwrites the political axis of Part III. The two matrices are homonyms whose meanings diverge on the one question that decides everything: where is the observer?

The Foulkesian Web

S. H. Foulkes (1898–1976), founder of group analysis, coined "matrix" for a wholly different object: "the hypothetical web of communication and relationship in a given group… the common shared ground which ultimately determines the meaning and significance of all events."21 The etymology is the Latin for womb — a generative medium, not a coordinate plane. On this account the individual is "a nodal point in a network of relationships," intelligible only through the web that suspends them.41 Where Stacey's matrix is a Cartesian grid read from outside, Foulkes's is a relational web in which the observer is a suspended node, with no outside and no coordinates.

The Shared Root: Elias

The comparison is structural, not coincidental, because the two matrices share a literal ancestor. Foulkes and Norbert Elias were friends from Frankfurt who, in London in 1952, co-founded the Group Analytic Society together.42 From Elias, Foulkes drew the foundational axiom that "the individual is the singular, and the social is the plural of the same phenomenon" — the Foulkesian matrix is Elias's figuration imported into the consulting room.43 And Elias equally grounds Stacey's complex responsive processes, and — as Parts III–IV showed — underwrites the political reading of the agreement axis. The same Eliasian source thus feeds the grid Stacey abandoned, the web he embraced, and the political dimension that is the diagram's neglected heart. This is why Stacey could rebuild on Foulkes after burning his own diagram: the web was already Eliasian, and so was he.

The Stacey Matrix (grid)The Foulkes Matrix (web)
FormCartesian grid, two axesNetwork, web of communication
Sense of "matrix"Mathematical — array of cellsLatin — womb, generative medium
The observerOutside, plotting coordinatesInside, a suspended nodal point
TimeA snapshotFoundation (inherited) + dynamic (woven)
Common ancestorNorbert Elias — figuration; the singular and plural of one phenomenon

The post-Foulkesian theorist Farhad Dalal — trained first in physics, drawing on chaos theory exactly as Stacey did — argued that there are two Foulkeses: an "orthodox" one who keeps the Freudian individual, and a "radical" one who follows Elias to dissolve the individual into the web.44 The structural rhyme is the deepest finding of the whole inquiry: each tradition contains a grid-self and a web-self, and Dalal performs on Foulkes the same Eliasian deconstruction that Griffin performs on Stacey. The truth in both lies in following Elias all the way and refusing the residual figure of the bounded observer that the grid expresses in its purest form.

The Irony Made Exact

The artefact the world calls "the Stacey Matrix" is the grid Stacey repudiated; the matrix concept he embraced — the web in which the observer is a node and meaning is the shared ground — bears another man's name. The grid was the error of his first phase; the web was the truth of his second. The popular reception canonised the error under his name while remaining wholly ignorant of the truth — and, as Parts III–VII showed, it did so precisely by deleting the one axis (agreement, the political, the entropic) that pointed beyond the grid toward the web.

The Third Mirror — The Matrix, the Advertisement, and the Self It Manufactures

The grid does not only stand against the web; it does something the web does not, and to name it we need a third mirror and a different discipline. For the four-box is not merely consulted — it is looked into, and what looks back is a flattering image of the organisation itself. The most exact account of how a flat image manufactures a self belongs to Judith Williamson, whose Decoding Advertisements (1978) took the advertisement apart as an ideological apparatus working, like the matrix, in what she called the apparently “neutral” realm of the signifier.76 Her method — undo the transparency of the seemingly innocent image and the politics it hides will show itself — is precisely the method of this study; and her central instrument is the one this Part has been circling: the mirror.

Williamson takes the mirror from Lacan. The infant, uncoordinated and unbounded, meets its reflection and recognises, in the unity of the image, a coherence it does not yet possess; it fastens onto that image and is thereby split, for to mean itself the image must be separate from itself. Lacan names the captured fictional totality the Ideal-Ego, and holds that the ego is constituted when the subject fastens onto an image that “alienates him from himself,” an ego clad at last in “the armour of an alienating identity.”77 The advertisement, Williamson shows, lives off exactly this: it holds out an idealised image of ourselves that we are invited to desire and can never become, a unity kept always at a spatial distance — and, in the faces that fill it, “a face stares back at us with a gaze that merges with our own.”76 The gaze that merges with our own: the phrase could be the motto of this entire study.

Hold the four-box up as such a mirror, and the organisation that looks into it sees not its real body — fragmented, conflictual, traversed by power and disagreement, an institutional thing flowing in all directions — but a unified gestalt: a rational, coordinated, masterful enterprise that knows which box it is in and what to do there. That smooth image is the organisation’s Ideal-Ego, and the diagram is the looking-glass that returns it. The reflection flatters precisely because it is armoured — sealed, coherent, without fracture — and the organisation fastens onto it, mistaking the coherent image for itself, in just the misrecognition (méconnaissance) Lacan described. What the four-box sells is therefore not a technique but an image of the manager’s own competence and control; and because the object of that desire is finally the self, the longing for the image becomes, in Williamson’s phrase, “the supply of power that drives the whole ad motor.”76 It is also the supply of power that drove the diagram across two hundred and forty fields (Part VIII): the four-box proliferated not because it is true but because it returns, to whoever holds it, a gratifying picture of themselves as the one who sees and sorts.

And the mirror addresses someone. Williamson’s second instrument, drawn from Althusser, is appellation: the advertisement’s mode of address — its “people like you” — hails a reader and, in hailing, constitutes them as a subject, so that ideology both forms and is formed by the category of the Subject.76 The matrix appellates too. It hails the manager — you, the one who surveys the field and assigns the kind; you, who decides whether this is simple or complex — and in hailing installs the manager in the subject-position of the sovereign sorter: the demon at the trapdoor (Part VII), the eye in the tower (Part VI). The “you” the four-box constructs is a manager who stands outside the organisation and reads it like a chart; and the manager, hailed, turns and becomes that “you,” taking up the diagram’s classifying gaze as their own — the same movement by which, in Part VI, the watcher moved inside the skull. The gaze that merges with our own is the diagram’s gaze, received as a neutral seeing.

But the mirror lies, and the lie carries the cost the earlier Parts have already named. The unity the diagram offers is an Ego-Ideal — the haunting after-image of a coherence that, Lacan insists, can never be regained once the subject has entered the order of difference, and whose pursuit is therefore replenished exactly because it is never fulfilled.77 The manager can no more become the sovereign, in-control sorter the four-box promises than the consumer can become the figure in the advertisement, because an organisation is irreducibly political and complex — irreducibly of what Lacan called the Symbolic, the order of difference and disagreement. So the chase has no end, and its name in the contemporary workplace is the one Part VI gave it: the achievement-subject optimising itself toward a managerial wholeness it can never reach, and burning out in the reaching. The flattering mirror and the self-exhausting subject are one phenomenon seen at two distances.

And now the deletion of the agreement axis can be read a final way — the way this Part was built to deliver. The agreement axis is the dimension on which an organisation’s real fragmentation — its disagreements, its distributions of power, its un-unified political body — would appear in the reflection. To delete it is to airbrush the crack out of the mirror: to take from the image precisely the line that would show the organisation its own division, leaving only the smooth, sealed, armoured unity for the subject to fasten onto. The four-box, stripped of agreement, is the looking-glass with the fracture retouched away — a mirror that returns an Imaginary whole where a Symbolic difference stood. This is why the mutation is not a simplification but a seduction: it perfects the diagram as a mirror by deleting the one axis that kept it a map. To restore the agreement axis is to restore the crack — to return the organisation from the Imaginary unity of the Ideal-Ego to the Symbolic order in which its differences are legible, contestable, and real. The web and the crack ask the same thing of the organisation: that it surrender the armour of the coherent image and consent to be seen as what it is — a thing made of relations and disagreements, with no single self to see and no single seat from which to see it.

Crux · The Mirror With the Crack Retouched Away

Williamson showed that an advertisement sells the viewer an idealised image of themselves and lives off the desire to become it. The four-box does the same to the organisation: it returns a flattering, unified reflection — rational, masterful, depoliticised — and the organisation fastens onto that Ideal-Ego, mistaking the armour for the body. The deletion of the agreement axis is what makes the reflection flawless, for it retouches away the one line on which the organisation’s real division would show. The whole of this study, seen through this last mirror, is an attempt to put the crack back — to make the diagram a map one can argue with again, rather than an image one can only ache to be.

◆
XI The Verdict · Part XI

Determinatio

"Life is complex all the time, not just on those occasions which can be characterised as being far from certainty and far from agreement." — Ralph Stacey

Set the parts together and the study resolves into a single argument, organised around the axis its imitators threw away. The Stacey Matrix was a low-entropy artefact — five precise zones, two distinct axes, a held-open paradox — built to stage the paradox of control. Its vertical axis, agreement, encodes the political: the irreducible fact that organisations are associations of competing interests, in which disagreement has no epistemic cure. That political axis is, at root, a measure of social entropy; the whole field the diagram maps is a non-equilibrium thermodynamic field, in which consensus is an improbable low-entropy order bought by the continuous work of power, and the zone of complexity is the far-from-equilibrium regime where order arises through amplified fluctuation. The diagram's far corner is not chaos but equilibrium — the social heat-death in which no work can be done.

The two documents that transmitted the diagram — Zimmerman's aide and the Praxis entry — preserved this structure faithfully, including the political zone and (in Zimmerman) the dynamic, observer-relative character and the continuity of the Larned diagonal. The decisive corruption, Schwaber's 2004 renaming, consists precisely in the deletion of the political axis — replacing an entropy variable with a second technical one — and from that deletion the 240-fold dissipation of the diagram's meaning follows. What its author kept, after burning the grid, was the Foulkesian web, descending from the same Eliasian root that underwrites the political axis itself.

The diagram that taught its lesson most widely was the one that could not survive its own lesson. Its honest centre — the admission that here the rational toolkit fails — names a far-from-equilibrium region the grid cannot map: the living web in which the observer is a node, the figuration that is no one's intention, the social order that must be worked into being against the entropy that would dissolve it. Determination of the present study

The right name for the artefact is neither "tool" nor "error" but threshold object: true only at the moment it points beyond itself, false the instant it is used to do the work it appears to offer. Its highest use is to deliver its reader to the threshold of the zone of complexity — to the recognition that the rational toolkit ends here — and then to be put down. To carry it across, to use it as a sorting grid in the region it marks as unmappable, is to mistake the map that says "here the map ends" for a map of what lies beyond.

Here the map says: the map ends. Its imitators printed over the warning.

And the deep unifier of the whole, the thread that ties the political to the physical, is entropy. Disagreement is entropy; consensus is negentropy bought by work; power is the capacity to do that work; complexity is the generative regime where entropy production and order rise together; and the diagram's own decline, across forty years and 240 variants, is itself an entropic dissipation — the decay of a low-entropy structure left without the maintenance of fidelity. The matrix is a map of how far an organisation sits from social equilibrium, and at what cost it is held there. Read so, it says what its author finally said in plain words: life is complex all the time — because the work of holding any human order away from its entropy never stops, in any zone, for anyone.

Notes & References

1. Stacey, R.D. (1993) Strategic Management and Organisational Dynamics, 1st ed. London: Pitman; (1996) Complexity and Creativity in Organizations. San Francisco: Berrett-Koehler.

2. Summarised at en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ralph_D._Stacey ("collapses the paradox of certainty and uncertainty… sustains the dominant discourse on management while using an alternative jargon of complexity").

3. Zimmerman, B. (2001) Ralph Stacey's Agreement & Certainty Matrix. Edgeware aide, Schulich School of Business, York University; hosted by the National Collaborating Centre for Methods and Tools (NCCMT), nccmt.ca. All quotations in Part II.A — the axis glosses, the political-zone passage, the "deliberate edge" move, the board-and-staff story, and the Larned diagonal with its seven interventions — are from this document.

4. Praxis Framework, "Stacey Matrix," praxisframework.org/en/library/stacey-matrix, citing Stacey, SMOD 3rd ed., Harlow: Prentice Hall, 2002 (the five zones as governance regimes; "Decision making becomes political rather than technical"; the WHOW-model mapping).

5. Cyert, R.M. & March, J.G. (1963) A Behavioral Theory of the Firm. Englewood Cliffs: Prentice-Hall (the firm as a coalition; goals as the product of internal bargaining and side-payments).

6. Thompson, J.D. (1967) Organizations in Action. New York: McGraw-Hill (the "dominant coalition" and "inner circle"; uncertainty multiplies power-bases and enlarges the coalition). On coalition stability and members' satisfaction: Murnighan & Brass (1991); Foss & Mazzelli (2025).

7. Elias, N. (1939/2000) The Civilizing Process; (1970) What is Sociology? (figuration; homines aperti; order from the interweaving of many intentions).

8. Schwaber, K. (2004) Agile Project Management with Scrum. Redmond: Microsoft Press, ch. 1 ("The Science of Scrum"), renaming the axes "Requirements Complexity" and "Technology Complexity."

9. Barth, S. (2023) analysis of where agile methods apply, LinkedIn (the "technocratic" reduction; loss of the interpersonal factor).

10. Rodgers, C. (2006) Informal Coalitions: Mastering the Hidden Dynamics of Organizational Change. Basingstoke: Palgrave Macmillan — organisations as competing political coalitions (after Eccles & Nohria 1992); the shadow side (after Egan 1993; Argyris on "undiscussability"); the coupling of political intensity to uncertainty and distance from agreement; functional politics and the four forms of power (instrumental, inner, relational, embedded); organisations as networks of self-organising conversations; culture as shared meaning-making. Draws explicitly on Stacey (2003).

11. Scott, J.C. (1990) Domination and the Arts of Resistance: Hidden Transcripts. New Haven: Yale University Press (public and hidden transcripts; the demythologising of "speaking truth to power").

12. Fleck, L. (1935/1979) Genesis and Development of a Scientific Fact. Chicago: University of Chicago Press (the thought collective and thought style; the stages of resistance to ideas contradicting the prevailing style).

13. Elias, N. & Scotson, J. (1965/1994) The Established and the Outsiders: A Sociological Inquiry into Community Problems. London: Sage (the "heroic we-identity"; praise and denigration gossip; policing against fraternisation).

14. Griffin, D. (2002) The Emergence of Leadership: Linking Self-Organization and Ethics. London: Routledge (systemic vs participative self-organisation; the "sleight of hand" of affirming an all-encompassing whole; "harmony and cult, caring and blaming").

15. Mowles, C. (2026) "‘DM me if you want to know who I'm really talking about,'" On Complexity (Substack), 14 May 2026 (gossip and the politics of status; the LinkedIn passage; the clinician's aside and the bullying-disclosure anecdotes).

16. On Peirce's doubt–belief account of inquiry ("belief, which is a form of stability and satisfaction"; doubt as organic irritation): Britannica, "Pragmatism: The classical pragmatists"; Peirce, C.S. (1877) "The Fixation of Belief."

17. Peirce, C.S., on abduction as "the first stage of inquiry"; see Koschmann, T. (2003) "Peirce's Notion of Abduction and Deweyan Inquiry."

18. Mead, G.H. (1934) Mind, Self and Society. Chicago: University of Chicago Press (gesture and response, the significant symbol, the I/me dialogue); Britannica, "Pragmatism: Other American pragmatists." The living present: Mead, The Philosophy of the Present (1932).

19. Dewey, J. (1938) Logic: The Theory of Inquiry (the indeterminate situation; the entanglement of means and ends); see "Inquiry, Meaning, and Organizing: A Pragmatist Take on Sensemaking," Springer (2025).

20. Stacey qualified as a group analyst at the Institute of Group Analysis, London, in 1998, and ran clinical therapy groups until c. 2004 (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ralph_D._Stacey).

21. Foulkes, S.H. (1964) Therapeutic Group Analysis. London: George Allen & Unwin (the group matrix as "the hypothetical web of communication and relationship in a given group"); Group Analytic Society, "The Group-Analytic Group Matrix Concept," groupanalyticsociety.co.uk.

22. On catharsis and "strangulated affect": Breuer, J. & Freud, S. (1895) Studies on Hysteria; see also the history of the cathartic method (Simply Psychology; Emotional Processing Therapy).

23. Catharsis as a therapeutic factor in group psychotherapy: Yalom, I. (1985) The Theory and Practice of Group Psychotherapy; Bemak & Young (1998) "Role of Catharsis in Group Psychotherapy"; StatPearls, "Group Therapy."

24. Prigogine, I. (1977) Nobel Prize in Chemistry; Prigogine, I. & Stengers, I. (1984) Order Out of Chaos. New York: Bantam.

25. The four conditions (openness, far-from-equilibrium, non-linearity, dissipation) as summarised in the dissipative-structures literature (e.g. Kondepudi & Prigogine, Modern Thermodynamics; arXiv reviews of dissipative systems, 2024–26).

26. Prigogine (1989), as quoted in the secondary literature ("increase in entropy is not an increase in disorder, for order and disorder are created simultaneously… the level of complexity rises as the generation of entropy, or dissipation, increases").

27. Prigogine & Stengers (1984: 140–1) ("certain fluctuations, instead of regressing, may be amplified and invade the entire system"); the Prigogine school's "order through fluctuations."

28. Bailey, K.D. (1990) Social Entropy Theory. Albany: SUNY Press; (1994) Sociology and the New Systems Theory.

29. Bailey, as summarised at en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kenneth_D._Bailey_(sociologist) (social entropy as "a measure of the natural decay of the structure or of the disappearance of distinctions within a social system").

30. Bailey (1990), the PILOTS/PISTOL framework and the social Gibbs free energy of "satisfaction or dissatisfaction with the social, political, and economic rules"; "discussion of order and power" (publisher synopsis; "The Social Entropy and Social Ecology," ResearchGate).

31. Shannon, C.E. (1948) "A Mathematical Theory of Communication," Bell System Technical Journal; H as the measure of uncertainty over a distribution of states. On the negentropy/free-energy correspondence: Brillouin (1953); Schrödinger (1944) What is Life?

32. Maxwell, C. (2022) It's Just Not That Simple: A Complex Adaptive Systems Approach to Understanding Changing Dynamics in Leadership Teams, PhD thesis, Faculty of Business, University of Technology Sydney. Five leadership teams subjected to quasi-experimental challenges and co-created sensemaking debriefs; the "Uncertainty Landscape" phase diagram; the theory of "complex dissipative teams" (metastable, far-from-equilibrium, decaying to equilibrium without sustained energy); ch. 2.4 "Concepts from thermodynamics: energy, work and entropy" and "We're envying the wrong physics"; ch. 6.2 "Complex dissipative teams" (equilibrium, entropy and decay, power in complex dynamics). Key finding: avoidance of the complex state yields "false consensus," whereas teams that sustain it "achieved greater consensus." Developed in dialogue with Chris Mowles.

33. Stacey, R.D. (1991) The Chaos Frontier. Oxford: Butterworth-Heinemann; (1992) Managing the Unknowable. San Francisco: Jossey-Bass.

34. Habermann, F. (2021) "Agile Populism or a Good Cause? The Example of the Stacey Matrix," overthefence.com.de / LinkedIn (the Schwaber renaming; von Bergen's "clarity of the goal and the way to get there" as "the crudest modification"; "homeopathic knowledge"; the "beam effect").

35. Stacey, R.D., Griffin, D. & Shaw, P. (2000) Complexity and Management. London: Routledge; Stacey & Mowles (2016) Strategic Management and Organisational Dynamics, 7th ed. Harlow: Pearson.

36. Stacey, R. (2009), cited via Informal Coalitions / S. Billing (2009), informalcoalitions.typepad.com ("life is complex all the time…").

37. Langton, C.G. (1990) "Computation at the Edge of Chaos: Phase Transitions and Emergent Computation," Physica D 42; PhD thesis, University of Michigan.

38. Kauffman, S. (1993) The Origins of Order; (1995) At Home in the Universe. Oxford University Press — "order for free"; the NK model of tunably rugged fitness landscapes (ruggedness rising with the interdependence parameter K; K = 0 a single smooth peak, K = N−1 maximally rugged). The adjacent possible developed in Kauffman (2000) Investigations, Oxford University Press; popularised in S. Johnson (2010) Where Good Ideas Come From, Riverhead.

39. Mitchell, M., Crutchfield, J.P. & Hraber, P.T. (1993) "Revisiting the Edge of Chaos: Evolving Cellular Automata to Perform Computations," Complex Systems 7: 89–130 (Santa Fe Institute).

40. Halpin, D. & Hanlon, N. (2008), on the Legitimate and Shadow systems and "bounded instability" at the edge of chaos (Irish Academy of Management).

41. Foulkes's "nodal point in a network of relationships"; the neurone/cortical-network analogy from Kurt Goldstein (Encyclopedia.com, "Foulkes (Fuchs), Siegmund Heinrich"); the matrix as womb / "transformational object" (Neupsy Key, 2021).

42. Group Analytic Society International, founded 1952 by Foulkes, Abercrombie and Elias (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Group_Analytic_Society).

43. Neupsy Key (2021) on Elias's influence on Foulkes ("the individual is the singular, and the social is the plural of the same phenomenon").

44. Dalal, F. (1998) Taking the Group Seriously: Towards a Post-Foulkesian Group Analytic Theory. London: Jessica Kingsley (orthodox vs radical Foulkes; the Eliasian basis); and Stacey (2003) Complexity and Group Processes, within the post-Foulkesian social-unconscious discourse alongside Dalal and Hopper.

45. Mowles, C. (2022) Complexity: A Key Idea for Business and Society. Abingdon: Routledge (Key Ideas in Business and Management) — on power in the organisational literature (Clegg et al. 2006; Fleming & Spicer 2014; the Foucault critique); the thought collective as ideology, quoting Fleck (1935/1981: 41); Mead's "cult values" (1914, 1932); Arendt on power, plurality and indeterminacy (1958); gossip and the shadow system after Scott (1990).

46. Weick, K.E. (1979) The Social Psychology of Organizing, 2nd ed. Reading, MA: Addison-Wesley. Organizing as "a consensually validated grammar for reducing equivocality by means of sensible interlocked behaviors"; equivocality and its reduction (ch. 1); consensual validation, grounded "in agreements concerning what is real and illusory" (after H. S. Sullivan); the double interact (after Allport, ch. 4); enactment — "people creating the environment that confronts them," who "repeatedly impose that which they later claim imposes on them" (ch. 6); selection as retrospective sense-making (ch. 7).

47. Wright, S. (1932) "The roles of mutation, inbreeding, crossbreeding and selection in evolution," Proceedings of the Sixth International Congress of Genetics 1: 356–366 — the original "fitness landscape," adaptation as movement across a surface of peaks and valleys.

48. Waddington, C.H. (1957) The Strategy of the Genes. London: Allen & Unwin — the epigenetic landscape; developmental trajectories as canalised valleys (chreods); the landscape shaped from beneath by a web of gene-staked "guy-ropes."

49. Juarrero, A. (1999) Dynamics in Action: Intentional Behavior as a Complex System. Cambridge, MA: MIT Press — the distinction between restrictive constraints and enabling constraints that, by ruling possibilities out, make new possibilities available.

50. Korzybski, A. (1933) Science and Sanity. Lakeville, CT: Institute of General Semantics — “a map is not the territory”; consciousness of abstracting; the impossibility of the self-containing map (after Royce); and the Whitehead epigraph, “a civilisation that cannot burst through its current abstractions is doomed to sterility.” First stated in his 1931 address to the American Mathematical Society.

51. Bateson, G. (1972) Steps to an Ecology of Mind; (1979) Mind and Nature — the infinite regress of maps (“the territory never gets in at all”); information as “a difference that makes a difference”; “we… select and edit the reality we see.”

52. Carroll, L. (1893) Sylvie and Bruno Concluded — the map “on the scale of a mile to the mile,” and the country used “as its own map.”

53. Baudrillard, J. (1981) Simulacra and Simulation — the precession of simulacra; the map that precedes and engenders the territory.

54. The Living Map: Improv, Reflexive Cartography and the Grammar of Immersive Process (2025), companion synthesis — every framework as “a theory of salience”; the Stacey Matrix as a map that renders power, relational history and emotional weather invisible; the immersion–abstraction paradox (after Stacey and Rodgers).

55. Raup, D.M. (1966) “Geometric analysis of shell coiling: general problems,” Journal of Paleontology 40 — the theoretical morphospace; the finding that most of the space of geometrically possible forms is empty, the empty regions marking developmental, constructional and ecological constraint.

56. Thompson, D’A.W. (1917) On Growth and Form. Cambridge University Press — the theory of transformations: the form of one organism derived from another’s by deformation of a coordinate grid.

57. Strogatz, S.H. (1994) Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos. Reading, MA: Addison-Wesley — phase space, trajectories, attractors and bifurcations; with H. E. Nusse & J. A. Yorke on border-collision bifurcations in piecewise-smooth maps (abrupt change as a state crosses a sharp border).

58. Zimmerman, B., Lindberg, C. & Plsek, P. (1998) Edgeware: Insights from Complexity Science for Health Care Leaders. Irving, TX: VHA Inc. — the “Layer 2” overlay of simple / complicated / complex / anarchy onto Stacey’s agreement–certainty matrix, with complexity as the largest region; the proximate origin of the simple–complicated–complex vocabulary.

59. Haraway, D. (1988) “Situated Knowledges: The Science Question in Feminism and the Privilege of Partial Perspective,” Feminist Studies 14(3) — the critique of the “god-trick” of the view from nowhere; “only partial perspective promises objective vision… answerable for what we learn how to see.”

60. Johnstone, K. (1979) Impro: Improvisation and the Theatre. London: Faber & Faber — the “offer” (“anything an actor says or does”); the improviser as “a man walking backwards.”

61. McGhee, G.R. (2007) The Geometry of Evolution: Adaptive Landscapes and Theoretical Morphospaces. Cambridge University Press — the distinction between empirical and theoretical morphospaces, and the constructional, functional and phylogenetic constraints that leave most of a morphospace unoccupied (developing the Raup–Seilacher tradition of constructional morphology).

62. Gould, S.J. & Lewontin, R.C. (1979) “The Spandrels of San Marco and the Panglossian Paradigm,” Proc. R. Soc. Lond. B 205; with Gould, S.J. & Vrba, E. (1982) “Exaptation — a missing term in the science of form,” Paleobiology 8 — spandrels as structural byproducts, and exaptation as the co-opting of a feature for a use it did not evolve to serve.

63. Hockney, D., with Weschler, L. — on the photographic “joiners” and the “moving focus,” and the lifelong rejection of one-point perspective (the “hell of one-point perspective” in which “you yourself are not even in the picture at all”; “800 [vanishing points]… the camera sees geometrically, but we see psychologically”). See D. Hockney, That’s the Way I See It (1993) and Secret Knowledge (2001); L. Weschler, “David Hockney’s Improbable Inspirations” (The Paris Review, 2018); Halcyon Gallery, “David Hockney’s Photocollages.”

64. Ryōan-ji (Peaceful Dragon Temple), Kyoto, late 15th c. — the kare-sansui dry garden of fifteen stones, arranged so that from any single seat on the hōjō veranda at least one stone is hidden, glossed by the temple as a figure of “our limited perspective”; built in part by kawaramono (riverbank labourers Kotarō and Hikojirō), whose names are carved on a half-buried stone; its meaning contested for five centuries. On its implicit visual structure see G. J. van Tonder & M. J. Lyons, “Visual structure of a Japanese Zen garden,” Nature 419 (2002).

65. Nietzsche, F. — perspectivism: “there are only interpretations” / “there are no facts, only interpretations” (Nachlass 1886; The Will to Power); and the positive account of objectivity, “the more eyes, different eyes, we can use to observe one thing, the more complete will our ‘objectivity’ be” (On the Genealogy of Morality, III §12).

66. Nagel, T. (1986) The View from Nowhere. Oxford University Press — objectivity as detachment, the attempt to view the world “from nowhere in particular and no form of life in particular”; with R. Rorty (1991), on replacing “the image of climbing out of our minds.”

67. On the impossibility of standing outside the frame: H. von Foerster (second-order cybernetics; “the environment as we perceive it is our invention”); G. Spencer-Brown, Laws of Form (1969), “to draw a distinction is already to stand on one side of it”; K. Gödel’s incompleteness theorems; and the Zen kōan “What was your face before your parents were born?” — as gathered in “Thinking About Thinking Outside the Box” (Harish’s Notebook, 2026).

68. On the politics of perspective — perspectives as learned, situated and power-laden framings rather than inherent facts; “perspectival seeing” and the rejection of an Archimedean point; and the ideological slant of institutional, media and algorithmic framing. See, among the sources surveyed, Perspectives on Politics (American Political Science Association / Cambridge University Press), and the literatures on perspectival political theory and on the factors that shape perspective gathered for this study.

69. Maxwell’s demon and the thermodynamics of information: J. C. Maxwell (1867; Theory of Heat, 1871) — the sorting demon; L. Szilard (1929), “On the Decrease of Entropy in a Thermodynamic System by the Intervention of Intelligent Beings”; R. Landauer (1961), “Irreversibility and Heat Generation in the Computing Process,” IBM J. Res. Dev. 5 (erasing one bit dissipates ≥ kT ln 2; “information is physical”); C. H. Bennett (1982), “The Thermodynamics of Computation,” Int. J. Theor. Phys. 21 (the demon’s memory and the cost of erasure). The maxim “no physical process has as its sole result the erasure of information” is after B. Schumacher.

70. Foucault, M. (1975) Discipline and Punish; (1963) The Birth of the Clinic — the medical and disciplinary gaze; the examination that “makes each individual a ‘case’… an object for a branch of knowledge and a hold for a branch of power”; Bentham’s panopticon and “visibility is a trap.”

71. Sartre, J.-P. (1943) Being and Nothingness — “the Look” (le regard), the gaze that objectifies the one it falls upon; and J. Berger (1972) Ways of Seeing — “we never look at just one thing; we are always looking at the relation between things and ourselves,” the surveyor and the surveyed (with L. Mulvey on the gaze as positioning).

72. Hacking, I. — “Making Up People” (1986; London Review of Books, 2006) and The Social Construction of What? (1999): dynamic nominalism and the looping effect of human kinds — “naming has real effects on people, and changes in people have real effects on subsequent classifications”; classification proceeds through five interacting elements (classification, people, institutions, knowledge, experts). Hacking names Nietzsche (The Gay Science, §58) the first dynamic nominalist. The Heisenberg remark (“what we observe is not nature itself, but nature exposed to our method of questioning”) is from Physics and Philosophy (1958).

73. Glouberman, S. & Zimmerman, B. (2002) Complicated and Complex Systems: What Would Successful Reform of Medicare Look Like? Discussion Paper No. 8, Commission on the Future of Health Care in Canada — the “cake / rocket / child” distinction (“the recipe is essential”; “sending one rocket increases assurance that the next will be OK”; “raising one child provides experience but no assurance of success with the next”); the lamplight metaphor; the emergency-room example; and the catalogue of system tensions (professional vs lay knowledge, evidence vs experience, rational planning vs self-organisation, and the rest).

74. Weick, K. E. (1995) Sensemaking in Organizations. Thousand Oaks: Sage — sensemaking as retrospective and “driven by plausibility rather than accuracy” (“facts are a nicety, not a necessity”); the parable of the soldiers lost in the Alps who escape using a map later found to be of the Pyrenees (“when you are lost, any map will do”), which Weick adapted from Miroslav Holub’s poem “Brief thoughts on maps” (Times Literary Supplement, 1977); and the signature question “how can I know what I think until I see what I say?” On the story’s contested provenance and moral, see T. Basbøll & H. Graham and the ensuing exchange.

75. Han, Byung-Chul (2010) The Burnout Society (Müdigkeitsgesellschaft; Eng. trans. Stanford, 2015) and Psychopolitics (2017) — the shift from the disciplinary society of negativity (the ‘should’) to the achievement society of positivity (the ‘can’); auto-exploitation (“the exploiter is simultaneously the exploited… more efficient than allo-exploitation, for the feeling of freedom attends it”); burnout and depression as “the direct pathologies of the achievement society”; and “even class struggle has transformed into an inner struggle against oneself.”

76. Judith Williamson, Decoding Advertisements: Ideology and Meaning in Advertising (London: Marion Boyars, 1978) — the advertisement as an ideological apparatus operating in the “apparently ‘neutral’ realm of the signifier”; appellation, by which an advertisement hails a reader and so constitutes a subject (after Althusser); the advertisement as a mirror that offers an idealised image of the self the viewer is made to desire; and the manufactured or ‘created’ self assembled through consumption.

77. Jacques Lacan, “The Mirror Stage as Formative of the Function of the I” (in Écrits, 1949/1966) — the Ideal-Ego formed by the imaginary capture of the ego in its reflection; the ego constituted as the subject fastens onto an image that “alienates him from himself” and assumes “the armour of an alienating identity”; the Ego-Ideal as the unattainable after-image of that lost unity; and the distinction between the Imaginary (sameness) and the Symbolic (difference). With Althusser’s “Ideology and Ideological State Apparatuses,” these are the instruments Williamson brings to the reading of advertising.

78. Pierre Bourdieu on classification and symbolic power — drawing on Distinction (1979), Language and Symbolic Power (1991) and Practical Reason (1994): classifications as “instruments — I should say weapons — and stakes in the struggle” (the lutte des classements); the imposition of “principles of vision and division”; symbolic violence as domination secured through the misrecognition (méconnaissance) of arbitrary categories as natural (doxa); and the power to name, of which the state holds “the monopoly of legitimate symbolic violence.”

79. Pierre Bourdieu on reflexivity — An Invitation to Reflexive Sociology (with Loïc Wacquant, 1992), “Participant Objectivation” (2003) and Pascalian Meditations (1997): the critique of “scholastic” or “theoreticist epistemocentrism,” the fantasy that thought can transcend its own social conditions; and the corrective demand to “objectify the objectifying subject” — to turn the tools of analysis back upon the classifier, dispossessing the knowing subject of the privilege it normally grants itself.

80. Loïc Wacquant, “Symbolic Power and Group-Making: On Pierre Bourdieu’s Reframing of Class,” Journal of Classical Sociology 13(2), 2013 — on group-making as a “sociosymbolic alchemy” that turns a mental construct into historical reality; the central problem of “the realization of categories”; the thesis that social groups “exist twice,” in the first-order objectivity of material distributions and the second-order objectivity of imposed classifications; the passage from a “class on paper” to a class in fact; the constitutive power of symbolic structures; and Bourdieu’s conceptual shift from ideology to symbolic violence. Wacquant notes that social science is itself drawn into group-making, its techniques borrowed by the powerful to project “a falsely rationalized vision of… rule” (the public sphere recast, in his phrase, as a “central bank of symbolic power”).

81. Pierre Bourdieu, Distinction: A Social Critique of the Judgement of Taste (1979) and On the State (Sur l’État, 2012): the reflexive maxim that “taste classifies, and it classifies the classifier”; and the account of the state, through the rites of institution, making official — and thereby socially magical — cuts in an otherwise continuous social reality.

◆
finis

"The diagram that taught its lesson most widely was the one that could not survive its own lesson." The agreement axis is the half of the map that asks something uncomfortable of power — and the half its imitators erased.

The Agreement Axis · A Critical Study of the Stacey Matrix, Its Sources, and the Thermodynamics of Disagreement
Built on the NCCMT / Zimmerman aide, the Praxis entry, Mowles's Complexity, Rodgers's Informal Coalitions · Prigogine · Bailey · Foulkes · Elias
Eleven parts, composed as a single argument · on the politics, power relations, and thermodynamics of the agreement axis
